nodes.label: QG
nodes.full_name: Quantum Gravity Frontier
nodes.description: The canonical instance of cross-architecture-non-sharing (one of the five structural-reason categories). QFT and GR are not jointly hosted by a single architecture; their organizing structures don't share at the architecture level. The gap is not an apparatus failure of one architecture extended to cross-arch scope — it is the sibling-non-sharing of two architectures whose organizing principles are internally coherent. Closure pathway: candidate-foundational apparatus from which both architectures emerge as different aspects of one deeper organizing structure. Multiple candidate-foundational families target this gap; none has discharged the unification.

architecture_level_edges.description: QFT does not span the QG gap; gauge-theoretic content with renormalization-group structure does not extend to gravitational content via QFT's own organizing principle.
GR does not span the QG gap; pseudo-Riemannian geometric content with general-covariance closure does not extend to quantum gauge content via GR's own organizing principle.

nodes.label: CC
nodes.full_name: Cosmological Constant Problem
nodes.description: Cross-architecture reach-termination involving QFT, GR, and stat mech jointly. Naive QFT vacuum-energy estimation gives ~$10^{120}$ above the empirically-required value $\\Lambda \\approx 10^{-122}$ in Planck units. SUSY cancellation has been largely ruled out by LHC results; anthropic landscape arguments are philosophically contested. The program's vacuum-energy in the program literature is highly discrepant with observation — the gap is documented as framework-boundary in program literature11.9. Multiple architectures contribute to the empirical content; none alone sustains the closure.

architecture_level_edges.description: QFT vacuum-energy estimation gives ~$10^{120}$ above observation; QFT alone does not sustain the empirical CC content.
GR's cosmological-term coupling does not derive its value; GR alone does not sustain why $\Lambda$ takes its observed value.

Statistical-mechanical equilibrium-vacuum content participates in the multi-architecture vacuum-energy structure; stat mech alone does not sustain the joint content. Secondary architecture (thinner edge in visual).

nodes.label: BH info
nodes.full_name: Black Hole Information Paradox
nodes.description: Cross-architecture non-sharing with bridge-failure aspects. Semi- classical gravity (QFT on curved background) predicts thermal Hawking radiation; unitary quantum mechanics requires information preservation. The two architectures impose apparently incompatible commitments at the BH evaporation regime. The island formula and replica wormhole results (Almheiri et al. 2019-2020) substantially improve the Page-curve story by deriving the entropy curve, but do not fully close the conceptual loop on microscopic information flow — what specific microscopic mechanism preserves information remains open.

architecture_level_edges.description: QFT-on-curved-spacetime sustains semiclassical Hawking radiation but the unitarity content of full QFT requires information preservation that the semiclassical articulation doesn't supply.
GR sustains BH horizon and singularity content; the semiclassical predictions appear to violate unitarity at the QFT level. The QFT-GR joint commitment is incompatible at this regime.

regime_content_loci.description: Empirical locus where the joint commitment is observable in principle (Hawking radiation spectrum, BH evaporation).
BH formation and evolution are within the classical-gravity regime, with quantum effects entering at the horizon.
Information preservation is articulated in QM-foundational language (unitary evolution); the paradox is at the boundary of QFT and QM-foundational commitments.

nodes.label: turbulence
nodes.full_name: Turbulence in Fluids
nodes.description: Multi-architecture interference content. Hosting architectures: classical mechanics + statistical mechanics + thermodynamics jointly, without any single architecture sustaining the whole. Empirical content is robust (Kolmogorov scaling, energy cascade, intermittency, transition to turbulence at critical Reynolds numbers) but theoretical articulation is partial — the Navier-Stokes equations sustain the dynamics formally, but the regularity/uniqueness of solutions is open (Clay Millennium problem). The proposed connection between physical turbulence and the categorical-mode "turbulence" content within architectures (active networks at $C \\gg 1$ in network-classification literature, multi-scale closure content) is candidate-and-speculative — a proposed structural analogy that has not been discharged.

equations.description: Navier-Stokes equation for incompressible fluid flow. The architecture's articulation; the regularity / uniqueness of solutions is open (Clay Millennium problem).

loose_ends.description: Whether smooth Navier-Stokes solutions exist globally for arbitrary smooth initial data is open. Singularities may form in finite time.

equations.description: Kolmogorov 5/3 scaling in inertial range. Empirically robust for high-Reynolds-number turbulence; the architectural articulation of multi-scale energy cascade.

loose_ends.description: Intermittency corrections to K41 scaling (multifractal formalism) provide partial articulation.

Status reflects empirical robustness (universal Kolmogorov scaling, intermittency) coexisting with open theoretical questions — most notably Clay Millennium Navier-Stokes regularity.

nodes.label: Koide formula
nodes.full_name: Koide Mass-Ratio Formula
nodes.description: Empirically substantial but architecturally unhosted. The Koide formula gives $\\frac{m_e + m_\\mu + m_\\tau}{(\\sqrt{m_e}+ \\sqrt{m_\\mu}+\\sqrt{m_\\tau})^2} = 2/3$ to ~5 decimal places using current PDG charged-lepton masses. The formula is empirically robust but architecturally unhosted — no accepted theoretical framework derives it, and proposed extensions (to quarks, neutrinos) have not closed. Sits at the totality-approach stratum because the formula is *of* the realized content (mass ratios) without belonging to any architecture's organizing structure.

equations.description: Koide formula for charged-lepton masses. Holds to ~5 decimal places using current Particle Data Group values. Empirically substantial; theoretically unhosted.

loose_ends.description: No accepted derivation. Proposed extensions to quarks and neutrinos have not produced equivalent precision.

nodes.label: muon g-2
nodes.full_name: Muon Anomalous Magnetic Moment
nodes.description: Candidate anomaly pending resolution. The muon anomalous magnetic moment $a_\\mu = (g_\\mu - 2)/2$ measured at Fermilab's Muon g-2 experiment shows a discrepancy with the SM theoretical prediction. The 2021 Fermilab result combined with BNL gave a ~4.2σ discrepancy relative to the Theory Initiative 2020 SM prediction (mainly using dispersive HVP). However, lattice-QCD HVP calculations (BMW 2021+) give SM predictions much closer to experiment, narrowing the interpretive space substantially. The discrepancy's status is currently provisional — not yet a stable empirical obstruction to the SM, but an open candidate for new physics.

equations.description: SM contributions to muon anomalous magnetic moment from QED, EW, hadronic vacuum polarization (HVP), and hadronic light-by-light (HLbL). HVP is the dominant uncertainty contributor.

loose_ends.description: Discrepancy between dispersive-HVP-based SM prediction (Theory Initiative 2020) and lattice-HVP (BMW 2021+). The two methods give inconsistent SM predictions; reconciling them is active investigation.

Status reflects provisional anomaly: Fermilab measurements (2021, 2023) discrepant with dispersive-HVP SM prediction, but BMW 2021+ lattice-HVP gives SM predictions much closer to experiment, narrowing the interpretive space.

nodes.label: ChPT
nodes.full_name: Chiral Perturbation Theory
nodes.description: Multi-architecture interference content as cross-scale bridge exemplar. Effective theory below QCD scale articulating low-energy hadronic physics (pion-pion scattering, kaon decays, hadronic structure) phenomenologically without first-principles QCD derivation. Empirically substantial (chiral perturbation theory predictions match experiment for low-energy QCD observables). Lattice methods provide partial discharge of the bridge from QCD to ChPT; analytic discharge is open. Sits at totality-approach because the EFT operates between QCD's regime-content and the empirical content it predicts, with the bridge from QCD to ChPT being partial.

bridges.bridge_status: partial (lattice QCD provides numerical discharge; analytic discharge open)

equations.description: Leading-order chiral Lagrangian for pseudoscalar mesons. Higher-order terms (Gasser-Leutwyler) systematically expand in $p^2/\\Lambda_\\chi^2$. The architecture's effective articulation; not derived from first-principles QCD.

loose_ends.description: The bridge from first-principles QCD to ChPT effective Lagrangian is partial. Lattice methods provide numerical discharge; analytic derivation remains open.

related_entries: qft (parent architecture)

nodes.placement_rationale: Placed at stratum 1.5 (cross-scale bridge) because chiral perturbation theory is an established effective theory operating between QCD's regime content and low-energy hadronic phenomenology, without first-principles QCD derivation. Totality-approach rather than regime-content-of-QFT because the EFT operates at a bridge with partial discharge — lattice provides numerical, analytic derivation remains open.

nodes.label: Hierarchy
nodes.full_name: Hierarchy Problem (electroweak / Planck scale)
nodes.description: Within-architecture frontier within QFT. The electroweak scale $v \\approx 246$ GeV is ~17 orders of magnitude below the Planck scale $M_{\\mathrm{Pl}} \\approx 10^{19}$ GeV. Quadratic divergences in the Higgs mass under loop corrections require fine-tuning to produce the observed $m_H \\approx 125$ GeV unless new physics enters at the TeV scale. SUSY at the TeV scale was the canonical candidate; LHC has now ruled out SUSY at TeV, narrowing the interpretive space. The frontier is structurally apparatus- mechanism-failure — the SM apparatus extrapolates to scales where the structural articulation fails to hold without unnatural cancellations.

architecture_level_edges.description: QFT's apparatus produces quadratic divergences in the Higgs mass requiring fine-tuning at SM scales; the within-architecture frontier.

regime_content_loci.description: The Higgs sector's $v = 246$ GeV scale is the empirical locus where the hierarchy problem is most directly articulated.
Electroweak sector content (W, Z masses, fermion masses) sits at the v scale; their hierarchy with $M_{\mathrm{Pl}}$ is the empirical content.

candidate_targeting.annotation: TeV-scale SUSY ruled out by LHC. SUSY at higher scales remains possible but loses the naturalness motivation. Not a direct candidate-foundational instance in v1's families but historically the canonical hierarchy-candidate.
Higgs as bound state of new strongly-interacting fermions; still active research with parameter constraints from LHC.
Warped extra dimensions can address hierarchy via geometric warp factor; constrained by LHC and precision EW.

If many universes with varying parameters exist (string landscape), anthropic selection produces $v$ value compatible with life. Philosophically contested and empirically untestable in conventional sense.
Relaxion mechanism: scalar field dynamically selects $v$ during inflation. Active research.

nodes.label: Strong CP
nodes.full_name: Strong CP Problem
nodes.description: Within-architecture frontier within QFT (QCD sector). The QCD Lagrangian admits a CP-violating term proportional to $\\bar\\theta_{\\mathrm{QCD}} G \\tilde G$, but experimental bounds from neutron electric dipole moment require $\\bar\\theta_{\\mathrm{QCD}} < 10^{-10}$. No QCD symmetry forbids the term; the smallness is unexplained. The frontier is structurally organizing-structure-failure within QCD — the organizing structure (gauge symmetry) doesn't constrain $\\bar\\theta_{\\mathrm{QCD}}$ to be small. Standard candidate: Peccei-Quinn symmetry producing a pseudo-Goldstone axion that dynamically relaxes $\\bar\\theta_{\\mathrm{QCD}}$ to zero.

architecture_level_edges.description: QFT's organizing structure (gauge symmetry alone) does not constrain $\bar\theta_{\mathrm{QCD}}$.

regime_content_loci.description: QCD sector is the empirical locus where the $G \tilde G$ term and the strong CP smallness are observed.

candidate_targeting.annotation: PQ symmetry provides dynamical relaxation; axion is observable target. Active experimental searches (ADMX, ABRACADABRA, IAXO). Theoretical mechanism substantial; experimental discharge gestural-to-partial pending detection.
Anthropic argument: small $\bar\theta$ required for stable matter. Philosophically contested.

candidate_targeting.program: Vafa-Witten 'No spontaneous CP violation in vector-like theories'
candidate_targeting.annotation: Theoretical constraint from vector-like nature of QCD; doesn't fully discharge the puzzle.

citations: Peccei-Quinn 1977 'CP Conservation in the Presence of Pseudoparticles'
Weinberg 1978 'A New Light Boson?' Phys Rev Lett 40:223
Wilczek 1978 'Problem of Strong P and T Invariance in the Presence of Instantons'

nodes.placement_rationale: Placed at stratum 4 (within-architecture frontier) because the strong CP problem is internal to QFT's QCD sector — the CP-violating $\\bar\\theta$ term is allowed by gauge symmetry but bounded by neutron-EDM experiments to ${<}10^{-10}$. Classified organizing-structure failure because the framework's organizing principle (gauge symmetry alone) doesn't constrain $\\bar\\theta$ to be small — calls for new structure (Peccei-Quinn), not mechanism refinement.

nodes.label: Flavor
nodes.full_name: Flavor Puzzle / Yukawa Hierarchy / CKM and PMNS Structure
nodes.description: Within-architecture frontier within QFT. The Yukawa coupling hierarchy spans ~6 orders of magnitude (electron Yukawa $y_e \\sim 10^{-6}$; top Yukawa $y_t \\sim 1$). The CKM matrix shows hierarchical mixing structure; the PMNS matrix shows different hierarchical structure. Why these specific values? Why these hierarchies? The structural-reason differs across candidate- foundational programs: in standard physics, organizing-structure- failure (QFT's organizing structure doesn't determine Yukawa magnitudes); in an open candidate-foundational program (per the program literature), bridge-failure (the framework's bare-to-observed bridge fails at functional-form level for charged-lepton Yukawa hierarchy). The frontier's structural-reason category depends on which candidate-foundational program is engaged.

architecture_level_edges.description: QFT's organizing structure determines the Yukawa Lagrangian's form but not the coupling magnitudes; bridge from bare to observed values is partially absent.

regime_content_loci.description: Electroweak sector contains the Yukawa structure; charged-lepton, quark, neutrino Yukawa hierarchies are empirical content.

candidate_targeting.annotation: Family symmetries with abelian or non-abelian structure produce Yukawa hierarchies via flavon VEVs.
GUT proposals predict Yukawa relations across quark/lepton sectors at unification scale.
Fermion overlap functions in extra dimensions produce hierarchies.
Statistical-distribution arguments suggest hierarchies as typical in random-Yukawa ensembles.

nodes.label: Measurement
nodes.full_name: Measurement Problem
nodes.description: Within-architecture frontier within QM-foundational. QM's organizing structure (unitary linear evolution between measurements) doesn't articulate why measurement events appear non-unitary (definite outcomes from superpositions). Schrödinger evolution describes physics between measurements; the Born rule articulates probabilities at measurement; but what *makes* measurement events different from other interactions is open. The frontier is structurally organizing-structure-failure: the architecture's organizing structure (unitarity) doesn't constrain measurement events. Multiple candidate programs propose different resolutions, with substantially different ontological commitments.

architecture_level_edges.description: QM's organizing structure (unitarity) doesn't articulate why measurement events appear non-unitary; within-architecture organizing-structure-failure.

regime_content_loci.description: Hilbert/measurement regime is where the Born rule articulates measurement probabilities empirically; the organizing-structure question lives here.

candidate_targeting.annotation: Unitarity intact; measurement is observer-relative branching. Substantial structural articulation; partial discharge of why we observe definite outcomes (decoherence + branching). Active research on quantitative theory.
Additional stochastic dynamics produces collapse. Empirically constrained; testable predictions. Partial discharge with phenomenological parameters.
Environmentally-induced superselection produces effective classicality. Substantial subclaim discharge; full ontological resolution partial.
Hidden-variable theory with non-local pilot wave. Substantial structural articulation; non-relativistic version well-developed; field-theoretic extension partial.
Operational/observer-relative interpretation; substantially philosophical; phenomenologically equivalent to standard QM.
MERA articulates ground states; doesn't directly address measurement problem but provides information-theoretic structure.
Information-theoretic axiomatic derivations of QM structure; partially address why-this-formalism question but not the measurement-event question directly.

Ghirardi-Rimini-Weber 1986 'Unified dynamics for microscopic and macroscopic systems'

Primary classification interpretive-underdetermination: QM admits multiple incompatible content-level readings (Many-worlds, collapse models, decoherence-and-branching, Bohmian, QBism) that agree on Born-rule statistics but differ on what wavefunctions, branching, collapse, and observer-relativity mean. Secondary classification organizing-structure-failure, because the framework's principle (linearity + Born rule) doesn't internally constrain what makes measurement events different from other interactions. Reading the two together: the principle is silent on the diagnostic and admits multiple consistent readings.

nodes.label: Matter-AM
nodes.full_name: Matter-Antimatter Asymmetry / Baryogenesis
nodes.description: Within-architecture frontier within QFT, with cosmological spillover to cosmological-models. The observed baryon asymmetry $\\eta = n_B / n_\\gamma \\approx 6 \\times 10^{-10}$ requires Sakharov's three conditions (baryon number violation, C and CP violation, departure from thermal equilibrium) to be satisfied. The Standard Model satisfies all three in principle but quantitatively insufficient — SM electroweak baryogenesis with SM CP violation produces asymmetry many orders of magnitude below observation. The frontier is structurally apparatus- mechanism-failure: the SM apparatus produces some asymmetry but extrapolates to insufficient magnitude.

architecture_level_edges.description: QFT's SM apparatus partially satisfies Sakharov conditions but quantitatively produces asymmetry well below observation.

regime_content_loci.description: Electroweak phase transition is the SM mechanism producing baryogenesis; partial discharge.
Empirical locus: $\eta_B$ measured via BBN and CMB.

candidate_targeting.annotation: Heavy right-handed neutrino decays produce lepton asymmetry, converted to baryon asymmetry via sphaleron processes. Substantial structural articulation; constrained by neutrino-mass observations.
Beyond-SM models (e.g., MSSM with light stop, two-Higgs-doublet) can enhance EW baryogenesis. Constrained by LHC.
Scalar-field condensates carrying baryon number decay to produce asymmetry. SUSY-motivated.
Heavy GUT-scale particles decay producing asymmetry. Constrained by inflationary dilution.

Structural-reason: apparatus-mechanism-failure (the SM apparatus doesn't produce a dark-matter candidate; new physics required) with the possibility that new physics could constitute a new architecture if dark matter has structurally novel content.

QCD axion (PQ) and ALPs (axion-like particles) provide candidates with motivation. Searches: ADMX, IAXO, ABRACADABRA. Connected to strong-cp-problem.
Right-handed neutrinos at keV scale could be DM; constrained by structure formation and X-ray observations.
PBH mass windows constrained by microlensing, CMB, gravitational waves; some windows remain.

Addresses small-scale structure puzzles (core-cusp, missing satellites).

nodes.label: NS regularity
nodes.full_name: Navier–Stokes Regularity (Clay Millennium Problem)
nodes.description: Clay Millennium Problem: do smooth solutions to the 3D incompressible Navier–Stokes equations exist globally for all smooth initial data, or can singularities form in finite time? The Navier–Stokes apparatus is precisely defined (Newtonian mechanics + viscous stress + incompressibility) and the equations sustain enormous empirical content (turbulence, fluid engineering, atmospheric dynamics) — but global regularity remains open as of 2024. Distinct from intractability of generic computations: the question is about analytic structure of the equations themselves.

architecture_level_edges.description: Navier-Stokes regularity is internal to classical fluid dynamics, which the map hosts under stat-mech for cross-scale articulations.

regime_content_loci.description: Empirical locus where the question becomes most pressing — fully-developed turbulence may reflect near-singular dynamics.

candidate_targeting.annotation: Demonstrates blow-up in a closely-related averaged system; specific to averaged equations, doesn't settle the original problem.
Conditional regularity results showing what controlled quantities would imply smoothness.
Large-scale simulations have not exhibited finite-time singularities but resolve only finite range.

Tao 2016 'Finite-time blowup for an averaged three-dimensional Navier-Stokes equation' J Amer Math Soc 29:601
Constantin-Fefferman 1993 'Direction of vorticity and the problem of global regularity' Indiana Univ Math J 42:775

Classified solvability failure because the apparatus is fully articulated and the equations are precisely defined — what's missing is an analytic proof (or constructive blow-up) for smooth solutions in 3D. Solvability failure rather than apparatus-mechanism failure because the failure isn't in any specific mechanism within the apparatus but in solving the equations as written; solvability failure rather than reach termination because no apparatus boundary is being crossed — the apparatus simply hasn't been solved.

nodes.label: Topological phases
nodes.full_name: Classification of topological phases of matter
nodes.description: Classification of topological phases of gapped quantum matter. The free-fermion case is largely solved by the ten-fold way (Altland-Zirnbauer / Schnyder-Ryu-Furusaki-Ludwig / Kitaev) and its crystalline-symmetry extensions, which give complete K-theory classifications across dimensions. The broader problem — interacting topological phases, fractional / symmetry-enriched / symmetry-protected topological order in strongly-correlated systems, and the classification of topological order with anyonic excitations — remains open. The structural reason is reach-termination: the K-theory machinery that closes the free-fermion case reaches its applicable limit at interactions, where the classification can collapse (e.g. ℤ → ℤ₈ for class BDI in 1d under interactions; Fidkowski-Kitaev 2010) or be enriched in ways the free-fermion apparatus cannot capture. New classification machinery (cobordism for invertible phases, fusion categories for topological order, generalized cohomology theories) is partially developed and partially overlapping but not yet closure-grade for the full interacting domain.
nodes.closure_condition: Awaiting: (i) classification machinery for interacting fermionic SPT phases that subsumes Fidkowski-Kitaev-type collapses; (ii) a complete classification of (2+1)d topological order beyond the modular tensor category framework's current reach; (iii) classification of symmetry-enriched topological order across symmetry groups and dimensions; (iv) integration of crystalline-symmetry, magnetic-symmetry, and internal-symmetry classification programs into a single framework.

candidate_targeting.annotation: Tensor-network / matrix-product-state methods provide classification machinery for (1+1)d SPT phases (Chen-Gu-Wen 2011 and subsequent work); 2d projected entangled pair state (PEPS) classification is partially developed. Discharge is partial — significant content classified but not closure-grade.

citations: Senthil 2015, Annu. Rev. Condens. Matter Phys. 6, 299 (review of SPT phases)
Wen 2017, Rev. Mod. Phys. 89, 041004 (review of topological order)
Chiu, Teo, Schnyder, Ryu 2016, Rev. Mod. Phys. 88, 035005 (review of free-fermion classification)
nodes.placement_rationale: Within-architecture frontier hosted by QFT (specifically the condensed-matter / many-body QFT sub-domain). Stratum 4 placement reflects that the frontier is internal to QFT rather than cross-architecture. Added in v14 because no condensed-matter frontier existed in v13 and the ten-fold way needed a bears-on target.

nodes.label: Ten-fold way
nodes.full_name: Altland-Zirnbauer / Schnyder-Ryu-Furusaki-Ludwig / Kitaev classification of topological phases of free fermions
nodes.description: Classification of gapped free-fermion Hamiltonians by their discrete symmetries (time-reversal T, particle-hole C, chiral S = T·C) into ten symmetry classes (the Altland-Zirnbauer ten), each of which carries a topological invariant — trivial (0), ℤ, ℤ₂, or 2ℤ — at each spatial dimension d, with a period-eight (real classes) and period-two (complex classes) Bott pattern across dimensions. The structure was identified for random-matrix ensembles by Altland and Zirnbauer (1997), extended to topological phases in 3d by Schnyder-Ryu-Furusaki-Ludwig (2008), and completed across all dimensions with the Bott-clock structure made explicit by Kitaev (2009). The 'periodic table' framing is Kitaev's.
nodes.domain_of_applicability: Gapped Hamiltonians of free (non-interacting) fermions in any spatial dimension d ≥ 0, with the three discrete symmetries T (anti-unitary, T² = ±1 or absent), C (anti-unitary particle-hole, C² = ±1 or absent), and S = T·C (unitary chiral, present iff both T and C present, or absent). Covers bulk topological invariants for crystalline systems in the absence of additional crystalline symmetries (with crystalline-symmetry extensions developed as a separate classification program). Excludes: interacting topological phases (where the classification can collapse or be enriched), fractional / symmetry-enriched topological order, topological order with anyonic excitations, gapless topological phases.

classification_axes.name: symmetry-class
classification_axes.description: The ten Altland-Zirnbauer symmetry classes, labeled by Cartan-class symbols. The class is determined by the triple (T², C², S-presence): A = (0,0,0); AIII = (0,0,1); AI = (+1,0,0); BDI = (+1,+1,1); D = (0,+1,0); DIII = (-1,+1,1); AII = (-1,0,0); CII = (-1,-1,1); C = (0,-1,0); CI = (+1,-1,1). Values listed in the canonical Bott-clock order used by Kitaev 2009 Table 1: complex classes (A, AIII) first, then the eight real classes in Bott-clock order starting from AI.

classification_axes.name: spatial-dimension
classification_axes.description: Spatial dimension of the gapped bulk. The classification extends to all d ≥ 0 with Bott-periodicity (period 8 for real classes, period 2 for complex classes); cells in this entry are populated for d ∈ {1, 2, 3} as the physically most-studied slice. Higher-dimensional content can be added without schema change.

cells.content: 0 (trivial)
cells.description: Class A in 1d: no nontrivial topological invariant.

cells.content: ℤ
cells.description: Class A in 2d: Chern-number-classified phases. The integer quantum Hall effect is the canonical realization.
realized_examples: integer quantum Hall effect (von Klitzing et al. 1980)
anomalous quantum Hall effect in magnetically-doped (Bi,Sb)₂Te₃ thin films (Chang et al. 2013)

cells.content: 0 (trivial)
cells.description: Class A in 3d: trivial. The Bott pattern for complex classes alternates ℤ–0 with period 2.

cells.content: ℤ
cells.description: Class AIII (chiral unitary) in 1d: ℤ-classified by a winding number.
realized_examples: Su-Schrieffer-Heeger (SSH) model (Su-Schrieffer-Heeger 1979) — theoretical paradigm; physical realizations in cold-atom and photonic lattices

cells.content: 0 (trivial)
ℤ
cells.description: Class AIII in 3d: ℤ-classified by a 3d winding number.

cells.content: 0 (trivial)
0 (trivial)
0 (trivial)
cells.description: Class AI (T² = +1, no particle-hole, no chiral) is trivial in d = 1, 2, 3. The first nontrivial entry occurs at d = 4 (ℤ).

cells.content: ℤ
cells.description: Class BDI (T² = +1, C² = +1, chiral) in 1d: ℤ-classified at the free-fermion level. The Kitaev chain (Kitaev 2001) is the canonical realization, with the winding number counting Majorana edge modes. Note: under interactions this classification collapses to ℤ₈ (Fidkowski-Kitaev 2010), a key example of the free-to-interacting reach-termination that motivates the open frontier.
realized_examples: Kitaev p-wave wire (Kitaev 2001) — theoretical paradigm

cells.content: 0 (trivial)
0 (trivial)
ℤ₂
cells.description: Class D (particle-hole, C² = +1, no time-reversal) in 1d: ℤ₂-classified, with the nontrivial sector hosting unpaired Majorana zero modes at boundaries. Predicted by Kitaev 2001 (as the spinless p-wave case of his model). Experimental claims of Majorana zero modes in semiconductor-superconductor heterostructures (Mourik et al. 2012 and follow-ups) have undergone significant revision, with notable retractions; the cell-level classification is theoretically uncontested but experimental confirmation has tension.
realized_examples: semiconductor nanowire / superconductor heterostructures — disputed (see Mourik et al. 2012, Albrecht et al. 2016, Zhang et al. retraction 2021)
vortex cores in topological-superconductor candidates (FeTe₀.₅₅Se₀.₄₅; Wang et al. 2018, also disputed)

cells.content: ℤ
cells.description: Class D in 2d: ℤ-classified topological superconductors. Sr₂RuO₄ was a long-standing candidate; the chiral p-wave interpretation is now considered disfavored by polarized-neutron-scattering experiments (Pustogow et al. 2019).
realized_examples: candidate p+ip topological superconductors (status revised)

cells.content: 0 (trivial)
ℤ₂
cells.description: Class DIII (T² = -1, C² = +1, chiral) in 1d: ℤ₂-classified time-reversal-invariant topological superconductor wires.

cells.content: ℤ₂
ℤ
cells.description: Class DIII in 3d: ℤ-classified time-reversal-invariant topological superconductor. Superfluid ³He-B is the canonical realization.
realized_examples: superfluid ³He-B (Volovik 2003; Qi-Hughes-Raghu-Zhang 2009)

cells.content: 0 (trivial)
ℤ₂
cells.description: Class AII (T² = -1, no particle-hole, no chiral) in 2d: ℤ₂-classified 'quantum spin Hall' topological insulators. The Kane-Mele model (2005) and BHZ model (Bernevig-Hughes-Zhang 2006) predicted the phase; experimental confirmation came in HgTe/CdTe quantum wells (König et al. 2007).
realized_examples: HgTe/CdTe quantum wells (König et al. 2007)
InAs/GaSb double quantum wells (Knez et al. 2011)
monolayer WTe₂ (Wu et al. 2018)

cells.content: ℤ₂
cells.description: Class AII in 3d: ℤ₂-classified 3D topological insulators. Predicted independently by Fu-Kane-Mele (2007), Moore-Balents (2007), and Roy (2009); confirmed first in Bi₁₋ₓSbₓ (Hsieh et al. 2008) and Bi₂Se₃ / Bi₂Te₃ / Sb₂Te₃ (Xia et al. 2009, Chen et al. 2009).
realized_examples: Bi₁₋ₓSbₓ (Hsieh et al. 2008)
Bi₂Se₃, Bi₂Te₃, Sb₂Te₃ family (Xia et al. 2009; Chen et al. 2009)

cells.content: 2ℤ
cells.description: Class CII (T² = -1, C² = -1, chiral) in 1d: 2ℤ-classified — the topological invariant takes even integer values.

cells.content: 0 (trivial)
ℤ₂
0 (trivial)
2ℤ
cells.description: Class C (particle-hole C² = -1, no time-reversal) in 2d: 2ℤ-classified. Singlet topological superconductors with broken time-reversal.

cells.content: 0 (trivial)
0 (trivial)
0 (trivial)
2ℤ
cells.description: Class CI (T² = +1, C² = -1, chiral) in 3d: 2ℤ-classified time-reversal-invariant singlet topological superconductor.

closure_status.description: Complete within the stated domain (gapped free-fermion Hamiltonians, three discrete symmetries, no further crystalline symmetries). Closure was established by Kitaev (2009) via the K-theory / Bott-periodicity argument, confirming and extending the d = 3 cases worked out by Schnyder-Ryu-Furusaki-Ludwig (2008). The 'within-domain' qualifier is load-bearing: closure does not extend to (a) interacting systems (where Fidkowski-Kitaev 2010 showed the BDI-d1 ℤ collapses to ℤ₈), (b) crystalline-symmetry-protected phases (a separate classification program), or (c) gapless topological phases.

predictive_yield.prediction: 2D quantum spin Hall insulator (class AII, d = 2, ℤ₂ classification) — a time-reversal-invariant insulator with helical edge states.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Kane-Mele 2005, Phys. Rev. Lett. 95, 226801; Bernevig-Hughes-Zhang 2006, Science 314, 1757 (HgTe-specific prediction)
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: König et al. 2007, Science 318, 766

predictive_yield.prediction: 3D topological insulator (class AII, d = 3, ℤ₂ classification) — a class of bulk-insulating materials with topologically-protected surface states.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Fu-Kane-Mele 2007, Phys. Rev. Lett. 98, 106803; Moore-Balents 2007, Phys. Rev. B 75, 121306; Roy 2009, Phys. Rev. B 79, 195322
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Hsieh et al. 2008, Nature 452, 970 (Bi₁₋ₓSbₓ); Xia et al. 2009, Nature Phys. 5, 398 (Bi₂Se₃)

predictive_yield.prediction: 1D topological superconductor wire (class D, d = 1, ℤ₂ classification) hosting Majorana zero modes at its endpoints.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Kitaev 2001, Phys.-Usp. 44, 131
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Mourik et al. 2012, Science 336, 1003 (initial claim); Zhang et al. retraction 2021, Nature 591, 354 (retraction of a high-profile follow-up); current status reviewed in Frolov 2021, Nature 592, 350 and Yu et al. 2021, Nature Physics 17, 482
predictive_yield.description: The theoretical prediction is firm and the classification cell is uncontested. Experimental status has substantial tension: several high-profile claims of zero-bias-peak signatures consistent with Majorana zero modes have been challenged or retracted; the field has not yet converged on a confirmed material realization. Status 'unconfirmed-tension' rather than 'confirmed' or 'falsified' reflects this honestly.

predictive_yield.prediction: 3D time-reversal-invariant topological superconductor (class DIII, d = 3, ℤ classification).
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Schnyder-Ryu-Furusaki-Ludwig 2008, Phys. Rev. B 78, 195125; Qi-Hughes-Raghu-Zhang 2009, Phys. Rev. Lett. 102, 187001
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Realized in superfluid ³He-B (longstanding; topological reading consolidated in Volovik 2003, 'The Universe in a Helium Droplet')

predictive_yield.prediction: Integer quantum Hall effect (class A, d = 2, ℤ classification) — quantized Hall conductance at integer multiples of e²/h.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Discovered 1980 (von Klitzing-Dorda-Pepper, Phys. Rev. Lett. 45, 494); topological interpretation via TKNN invariant (Thouless-Kohmoto-Nightingale-den Nijs 1982, Phys. Rev. Lett. 49, 405); class A / ten-fold-way framing applied retrospectively.
predictive_yield.description: Recorded as retro-explanatory-only because the experimental phenomenon predates the ten-fold-way framework. The classification correctly hosts the IQHE; it did not predict it.

Schnyder, Ryu, Furusaki, Ludwig 2008, Phys. Rev. B 78, 195125 (classification of topological insulators and superconductors in 3d)

nodes.label: SM rep content
nodes.full_name: Standard Model gauge-group representation content
nodes.description: The Standard Model's particle content, specified as the representations carried by the SM matter fields, gauge bosons, and Higgs doublet under the gauge group G_SM = SU(3)_c × SU(2)_L × U(1)_Y, prior to electroweak symmetry breaking. The classification is the structural specification of which gauge-rep multiplets nature populates with fundamental fields, and at what multiplicity (three generations for fermions; one each for gauge bosons and the Higgs). Closed empirically with the Higgs discovery (2012); before that, the (1, 2, +1/2) scalar cell carried a predicted-but-unobserved status for 48 years. The classification is famously rep-theoretically constrained — the requirement of gauge-anomaly cancellation forces the hypercharge assignments across the matter content to satisfy non-trivial relations, which is what made charm (Glashow-Iliopoulos-Maiani 1970) and the existence of a third generation top-quark predictions rather than fits.
nodes.domain_of_applicability: The Standard Model gauge group SU(3)_c × SU(2)_L × U(1)_Y and the field content it constrains. Includes: quark and lepton multiplets across three generations; SU(3)_c, SU(2)_L, U(1)_Y gauge bosons; the Higgs scalar doublet. Excludes: right-handed neutrinos (not part of the strict SM; would be (1, 1, 0) singlets and are added in many SM extensions for neutrino mass); gravitational interactions; physics above the EW scale where new gauge structure may exist; the Yukawa coupling values themselves (the rep content fixes which Yukawa couplings exist; their numerical values are separately measured and constitute the flavor-puzzle frontier).
nodes.internal_structure: Each multiplet's gauge representation under G_SM = SU(3)_c × SU(2)_L × U(1)_Y, in the convention where U(1)_Y is normalized so that electric charge Q = T_3 + Y: Q_L → (3, 2, +1/6); u_R → (3, 1, +2/3); d_R → (3-bar, 1, -1/3) [or equivalently the conjugate (3, 1, -1/3) depending on convention]; L_L → (1, 2, -1/2); e_R → (1, 1, -1); gluons → (8, 1, 0); W → (1, 3, 0); B → (1, 1, 0); H → (1, 2, +1/2). The anomaly-cancellation constraint Tr[Y³] = 0 + Tr[Y · T_a²] = 0 + gauge-gravitational consistency is satisfied non-trivially across the matter content per generation; this constraint is what made GIM's prediction of charm and Glashow-Iliopoulos-Maiani's structural argument possible.

classification_axes.name: particle-multiplet
classification_axes.description: The nine SM multiplets, in the canonical order: left-handed quark doublet; right-handed up-quark singlet; right-handed down-quark singlet; left-handed lepton doublet; right-handed charged-lepton singlet; SU(3)_c gauge bosons (eight gluons); SU(2)_L gauge bosons (three W's); U(1)_Y gauge boson (one B); Higgs scalar doublet. Each multiplet carries a specific G_SM representation; the reps are recorded in the node's internal_structure field rather than as a separate axis because the SM's structural claim is that nature populates exactly these reps and no others (i.e. the rep is metadata about each multiplet, not a free axis along which the classification varies).
classification_axes.citation: Particle Data Group, Review of Particle Physics, latest revision (live document at pdg.lbl.gov); see also Weinberg 1996, The Quantum Theory of Fields Vol. II, §21.3

classification_axes.name: generation
classification_axes.description: Generation index for fermion multiplets (1, 2, 3); 'N/A' for multiplets that do not replicate over generations (the gauge bosons and the Higgs doublet). The SM's structural claim that gauge bosons and the Higgs are gauge-group-determined and do not replicate, while fermions appear in three identical-rep copies with hugely different Yukawa couplings, is captured by the asymmetry of which (multiplet, generation) combinations are real versus structurally-excluded.

cells.description: The Higgs scalar field. Vacuum expectation value v ≈ 246 GeV breaks SU(2)_L × U(1)_Y → U(1)_EM. Predicted in 1964 (Englert-Brout, Higgs); observed in 2012 at the LHC. The cell carried a 'predicted-but-unobserved' status for 48 years.

predictions.prediction_citation: Englert, Brout 1964, Phys. Rev. Lett. 13, 321; Higgs 1964, Phys. Rev. Lett. 13, 508; Guralnik, Hagen, Kibble 1964, Phys. Rev. Lett. 13, 585
predictions.confirmation_citation: ATLAS Collaboration 2012, Phys. Lett. B 716, 1; CMS Collaboration 2012, Phys. Lett. B 716, 30 (Higgs observation at the LHC, 125 GeV)

closure_status.description: Complete as a specification of G_SM representations carried by the matter, gauge, and scalar fields of the strict Standard Model. Closure was empirically completed in 2012 with the Higgs discovery; the rep content has been the SM's stable structural specification since Glashow-Weinberg-Salam-GIM completed the gauge structure in the early 1970s. 'Within-domain' qualifier is load-bearing: closure does not extend to (i) whether G_SM is the correct organizing gauge structure at energies above the EW scale (GUT-scale extensions add new gauge content), (ii) why there are exactly three generations (number not predicted, only the consistency of any given number is), (iii) whether right-handed neutrinos exist (not included in the strict SM but added in see-saw and other extensions for neutrino mass), (iv) gravitational interactions.

predictive_yield.description: The most cell-specific Higgs prediction had a 48-year wait from theoretical proposal to experimental confirmation. The mass was not predicted by the rep content alone (the Higgs self-coupling that fixes m_H is a free parameter of the SM); the mass-eigenvalue at 125 GeV was unknown until 2012.

predictive_yield.prediction: Existence of antiparticles (the conjugate-rep components — d_L is paired with d_L-bar; the SM rep content implicitly specifies the antimatter sector).
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Dirac 1928, Proc. R. Soc. A 117, 610 (the Dirac equation predicts antimatter prior to the SM)
predictive_yield.description: Antimatter predates the SM. The SM rep content makes antimatter a structural feature (each chirality has a conjugate rep), but the prediction is Dirac's, not the SM's.

nodes.label: PDG
nodes.full_name: Particle Data Group
nodes.description: Standing international collaboration that has curated the Standard Model particle table and its experimental status continuously since 1957. The Review of Particle Physics, published biennially (latest issues annually online), is the canonical reference for particle properties, decay modes, and cross-section measurements across all SM-relevant experimental programs.
key_publications: Workman et al. (Particle Data Group) 2022, Prog. Theor. Exp. Phys. 2022, 083C01 (current Review of Particle Physics)
Patrignani et al. (PDG) 2016, Chin. Phys. C 40, 100001
Olive et al. (PDG) 2014, Chin. Phys. C 38, 090001
citations: Workman et al. (Particle Data Group) 2022, Prog. Theor. Exp. Phys. 2022, 083C01

nodes.label: ATLAS + CMS at LHC
nodes.full_name: ATLAS and CMS Collaborations at CERN's Large Hadron Collider
nodes.description: The two principal general-purpose particle physics experiments at CERN's Large Hadron Collider, jointly discovering the Higgs boson in 2012 and continuing to study Higgs properties, top-quark physics, and searches for physics beyond the SM. ATLAS and CMS are independent collaborations with independent detectors; their joint discovery of the Higgs (announced simultaneously on 4 July 2012) is the canonical case of multi-collaboration confirmation in particle physics.
key_publications: ATLAS Collaboration 2012, Phys. Lett. B 716, 1 (Higgs observation)
CMS Collaboration 2012, Phys. Lett. B 716, 30 (Higgs observation)
ATLAS + CMS Collaborations 2016, JHEP 08, 045 (combined Higgs measurements)

nodes.label: UA1 + UA2 at SPS
nodes.full_name: UA1 and UA2 Collaborations at CERN's Super Proton Synchrotron
nodes.description: Two complementary experiments at CERN's SPS proton-antiproton collider in the early 1980s that jointly discovered the W and Z bosons, confirming the electroweak unification predicted by Glashow, Weinberg, and Salam. Operating concurrently with somewhat different detector technologies, the two collaborations provided independent cross-checks of the discovery.
key_publications: Arnison et al. (UA1) 1983, Phys. Lett. B 122, 103 (W± discovery)
Bagnaia et al. (UA2) 1983, Phys. Lett. B 129, 130 (W± discovery)
Arnison et al. (UA1) 1983, Phys. Lett. B 126, 398 (Z discovery)
Banner et al. (UA2) 1983, Phys. Lett. B 129, 130 (Z discovery)
citations: Arnison et al. (UA1) 1983, Phys. Lett. B 122, 103
Bagnaia et al. (UA2) 1983, Phys. Lett. B 129, 130

nodes.label: CDF + D0 at Tevatron
nodes.full_name: CDF and D0 Collaborations at Fermilab's Tevatron
nodes.description: Two general-purpose proton-antiproton collider experiments at Fermilab's Tevatron (1985-2011), jointly discovering the top quark in 1995. Operating concurrently like UA1/UA2 a decade earlier, CDF and D0 provided independent confirmation of the top-quark observation through their respective detectors and analyses. Tevatron operations ended in 2011 with the rise of the LHC, but the collaborations have continued to publish legacy analyses.
key_publications: Abe et al. (CDF) 1995, Phys. Rev. Lett. 74, 2626 (top quark observation)
Abachi et al. (D0) 1995, Phys. Rev. Lett. 74, 2632 (top quark observation)
Tevatron Electroweak Working Group 2014, arXiv:1407.2682 (final combined top-quark mass)
citations: Abe et al. (CDF) 1995, Phys. Rev. Lett. 74, 2626
Abachi et al. (D0) 1995, Phys. Rev. Lett. 74, 2632

nodes.label: DONUT
nodes.full_name: Direct Observation of NU Tau Collaboration
nodes.description: Fermilab experiment that achieved the first direct observation of tau neutrino interactions in 2000, completing the experimental confirmation of the three-generation lepton structure of the Standard Model. The experiment exposed emulsion targets to a tau-neutrino-enriched beam derived from charm-meson decays, then scanned for the characteristic tau-decay tracks emerging from neutrino interactions.
key_publications: Kodama et al. (DONUT) 2001, Phys. Lett. B 504, 218 (tau neutrino observation)
citations: Kodama et al. (DONUT) 2001, Phys. Lett. B 504, 218

nodes.label: Brookhaven + SLAC (J/ψ)
nodes.full_name: Brookhaven AGS (Ting group) and SLAC SPEAR (Richter group) — November Revolution
nodes.description: Independent simultaneous discovery of the J/ψ meson in 1974 at Brookhaven National Laboratory (Ting and collaborators, calling it 'J') and at SLAC's SPEAR storage ring (Richter and collaborators, calling it 'ψ'). The discovery, joint-named J/ψ, confirmed the existence of the charm quark predicted by GIM (1970), establishing the second-generation up-type quark and triggering the rapid acceptance of the four-quark Standard Model. Known as the 'November Revolution' for the speed at which the discovery reshaped the field.
key_publications: Aubert et al. (Brookhaven E598) 1974, Phys. Rev. Lett. 33, 1404 (J observation)
Augustin et al. (SLAC-LBL Mark I) 1974, Phys. Rev. Lett. 33, 1406 (ψ observation)
citations: Aubert et al. 1974, Phys. Rev. Lett. 33, 1404
Augustin et al. 1974, Phys. Rev. Lett. 33, 1406

nodes.label: Fermilab E288 (Upsilon)
nodes.full_name: Fermilab E288 Collaboration (Lederman group)
nodes.description: Fermilab fixed-target experiment that discovered the Upsilon (Υ) meson, a bottomonium bound state, in 1977. The discovery established the existence of the bottom quark, the third-generation down-type quark required by the Kobayashi-Maskawa three-generation CP-violation structure.
key_publications: Herb et al. (E288) 1977, Phys. Rev. Lett. 39, 252 (Upsilon discovery)
citations: Herb et al. 1977, Phys. Rev. Lett. 39, 252

nodes.label: ADE Lie algebras
nodes.full_name: Simply-laced finite-dimensional complex simple Lie algebras (Killing-Cartan ADE)
nodes.description: The classification of simply-laced finite-dimensional complex simple Lie algebras: the two infinite series A_n = sl(n+1, ℂ) for n ≥ 1 and D_n = so(2n, ℂ) for n ≥ 4, plus the three exceptional algebras E_6, E_7, E_8. 'Simply-laced' means all roots have the same length, equivalently the Dynkin diagram has only single edges. The classification is the most famous and structurally productive instance of the ADE pattern in mathematics; the other ADE classifications in this project (finite SU(2) subgroups, Du Val singularities, SU(2)_k WZW modular invariants, representation-finite quivers) all correspond to the ADE Lie algebras through structured bijections, formalized in v15 as typed cross-classification edges.
nodes.domain_of_applicability: Finite-dimensional complex simple Lie algebras with simply-laced root systems. Excludes: non-simply-laced simple Lie algebras (B_n, C_n, F_4, G_2); infinite-dimensional Lie algebras (affine and Kac-Moody); Lie superalgebras; Lie algebras over non-algebraically-closed fields; loop algebras.
nodes.internal_structure: Each Cartan type has a Dynkin diagram: A_n is a path of n nodes; D_n is a path of n-2 nodes with two extra nodes attached at one end (a 'fork'); E_6, E_7, E_8 are 'forks' with one short, one medium, and one long branch of total node-counts 6, 7, 8 respectively.

classification_axes.name: cartan-type
classification_axes.description: The five Cartan-type labels of the simply-laced finite-dimensional simple Lie algebras.

cells.content: A_n = sl(n+1, ℂ) for n ≥ 1; rank n; dim n(n+2)
realized_examples: A_2 = sl(3,ℂ) — SU(3) flavor/color
A_4 = sl(5,ℂ) — SU(5) GUT

cells.content: D_n = so(2n, ℂ) for n ≥ 4; rank n; dim n(2n-1)
realized_examples: D_4 = so(8) — triality, 8d spinors
D_5 = so(10) — Fritzsch-Minkowski-Georgi GUT

cells.content: E_6; rank 6; dim 78; fundamental 27-dimensional rep
realized_examples: E_6 GUT (Gürsey-Ramond-Sikivie 1976)

cells.content: E_7; rank 7; dim 133; fundamental 56-dimensional rep
realized_examples: N=8 supergravity in 4d (Cremmer-Julia 1979) — E_{7(7)} hidden symmetry

cells.content: E_8; rank 8; dim 248; adjoint is smallest non-trivial rep; root lattice = E_8 lattice
realized_examples: E_8 × E_8 heterotic string
E_8 in Ising-in-field (Zamolodchikov 1989; Coldea et al. 2010)

closure_status.description: Complete. Established by Killing (1888-1890), refined by Cartan (1894).

predictive_yield.prediction: E_8 spectrum in Ising-in-field at criticality: eight stable particles with mass ratios determined by the E_8 root system.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Zamolodchikov 1989, Int. J. Mod. Phys. A 4, 4235
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Coldea et al. 2010, Science 327, 177

citations: Humphreys 1972, Introduction to Lie Algebras and Representation Theory (Springer GTM 9)
Bourbaki 1968, Groupes et algèbres de Lie, Chapitres 4-6

nodes.label: ADE finite SU(2) subgroups
nodes.full_name: Finite subgroups of SU(2) (Klein 1884)
nodes.description: The classification of finite subgroups of SU(2) up to conjugation. Two infinite series (cyclic Z_n and binary dihedral BD_{4n}) plus three sporadic cases (binary tetrahedral 2T, binary octahedral 2O, binary icosahedral 2I). The McKay correspondence (McKay 1980) gives a bijection between these finite SU(2) subgroups and the ADE Dynkin diagrams.
nodes.domain_of_applicability: Finite subgroups of SU(2), equivalently finite subgroups of the unit quaternions S³. Excludes: continuous SU(2) subgroups; finite subgroups of higher SU(N); finite subgroups of SO(3) directly.
nodes.internal_structure: Each finite SU(2) subgroup Γ acts on ℂ² by its defining representation. The McKay quiver of Γ is built by taking the irreducible representations of Γ as vertices and drawing edges according to the decomposition of (2-dim defining rep) ⊗ (irrep V_i) into irreps.

classification_axes.name: cartan-type
classification_axes.description: ADE labels via the McKay correspondence.
classification_axes.citation: McKay 1980, in Proc. Sympos. Pure Math. 37, 183

cells.content: Cyclic groups Z_{n+1} of order n+1; abelian
Binary dihedral BD_{4(n-2)} of order 4(n-2) for n ≥ 4; non-abelian
Binary tetrahedral 2T of order 24
Binary octahedral 2O of order 48
Binary icosahedral 2I of order 120

closure_status.description: Complete. Klein (1884) classified the finite rotation groups of the sphere; McKay (1980) recognized the ADE correspondence.

citations: Klein 1884, Vorlesungen über das Ikosaeder und die Auflösung der Gleichungen vom fünften Grade
McKay 1980, in Proc. Sympos. Pure Math. 37, 183

nodes.label: ADE Du Val singularities
nodes.full_name: Du Val / Kleinian / rational double point surface singularities (ADE)
nodes.description: The classification of isolated rational double points on complex algebraic surfaces, equivalently quotient singularities ℂ²/Γ where Γ ⊂ SU(2) is finite. The resolution attaches a configuration of (-2)-curves whose intersection graph is the corresponding ADE Dynkin diagram.
nodes.domain_of_applicability: Isolated rational double points on complex algebraic or analytic surfaces. Excludes: higher-dimensional singularities; non-rational singularities; non-quotient singularities.

classification_axes.name: cartan-type
classification_axes.description: ADE Cartan type.

cells.content: D_n: x²y + y^{n-1} + z² = 0 for n ≥ 4; ℂ²/BD_{4(n-2)}
E_6: x⁴ + y³ + z² = 0; ℂ²/2T
E_7: x³y + y³ + z² = 0; ℂ²/2O
E_8: x⁵ + y³ + z² = 0; ℂ²/2I

closure_status.description: Complete. Du Val (1934), Brieskorn (1966, 1968), Slodowy (1980).

citations: Du Val 1934, Proc. Cambridge Phil. Soc. 30, 453, 460, 483
Brieskorn 1966, Math. Ann. 166, 76 and 1968, Actes Congr. Internat. Math. Nice 2, 279
Slodowy 1980, Simple Singularities and Simple Algebraic Groups (Springer LNM 815)

nodes.label: ADE SU(2)_k modular invariants
nodes.full_name: Cappelli-Itzykson-Zuber classification of modular invariants of the SU(2)_k WZW model
nodes.description: The classification of modular-invariant partition functions for the SU(2) Wess-Zumino-Witten conformal field theory at level k. The A-series invariant exists at all levels; D-series at even levels; E-series at sporadic single levels (k = 10, 16, 28). v15 retrofit: level axis values now typed via axis_value_ranges (A, D) or single integers (E_6, E_7, E_8), replacing the v14 string-encoded workaround.
nodes.domain_of_applicability: Modular-invariant partition functions for the SU(2)_k WZW model in 2D rational conformal field theory.

classification_axes.name: cartan-type
classification_axes.description: ADE Cartan type of the modular invariant.

classification_axes.name: level
classification_axes.description: WZW level k (positive integer). v15: cells specify level coverage via axis_values (single point) or axis_value_ranges (ranges).

cells.content: Diagonal modular invariant Z_A = Σ_{λ=0}^{k} |χ_λ|²; labeled A_{k+1}
cells.description: The diagonal modular invariant is the universal partition function present at every level. v15 retrofit: level coverage now typed as axis_value_range with kind=all.

cells.content: Z_2-orbifold modular invariant of the diagonal; labeled D_{k/2+1}
cells.description: The D-series invariants are Z_2 orbifolds of the A-series at even level. v15 retrofit: level coverage now typed as axis_value_range with kind=every-nth, n=2, offset=0, min=4.

cells.content: Exceptional modular invariant tied to su(2) ⊂ sp(4); exists only at level k = 10
cells.description: Sporadic E_6 modular invariant. v15 retrofit: level is now an integer (10), not a string.

cells.content: Exceptional modular invariant; exists only at level k = 16
cells.description: Sporadic E_7 modular invariant. v15 retrofit: level is now integer (16).

cells.content: Exceptional modular invariant tied to su(2) ⊂ E_8 at level 1; exists only at level k = 28
cells.description: Sporadic E_8 modular invariant. v15 retrofit: level is now integer (28). The level k=28 corresponds to the dual Coxeter number of E_8 minus 2.

closure_status.description: Complete for SU(2)_k. CIZ 1987; Gannon 2000.

citations: Cappelli, Itzykson, Zuber 1987, Nucl. Phys. B 280, 445 and Commun. Math. Phys. 113, 1
Di Francesco, Mathieu, Sénéchal 1997, Conformal Field Theory, Springer, Ch. 17

nodes.label: ADE quivers (Gabriel)
nodes.full_name: Connected representation-finite quivers (Gabriel's theorem)
nodes.description: Gabriel's theorem (1972): a connected quiver has finitely many indecomposable finite-dim representations iff its underlying graph is an ADE Dynkin diagram. The indecomposable representations are in bijection with positive roots of the corresponding ADE root system.
nodes.domain_of_applicability: Connected finite quivers and their finite-dimensional representations over an algebraically closed field. Excludes: infinite quivers; quivers with relations; non-connected quivers.

classification_axes.name: cartan-type
classification_axes.description: ADE Cartan type; underlying graph of the quiver.

cells.content: A_n linear quiver, n vertices; n(n+1)/2 indecomposables (interval modules)
D_n quiver, n vertices for n ≥ 4; n(n-1) indecomposables
E_6 quiver; 36 indecomposables
E_7 quiver; 63 indecomposables
E_8 quiver; 120 indecomposables (matching positive root count)

closure_status.description: Complete. Gabriel 1972; Bernstein-Gelfand-Ponomarev 1973.

citations: Bernstein, Gelfand, Ponomarev 1973, Russian Math. Surveys 28, 17
Assem, Simson, Skowroński 2006, Elements of the Representation Theory of Associative Algebras, Vol. 1 (Cambridge)

nodes.label: Spacetime symmetry groups
nodes.full_name: Classification of spacetime symmetry groups used in physical theories
nodes.description: The classification of symmetry groups that act on spacetime in physical theories: the Galilean group of Newtonian spacetime, the Poincaré group of Minkowski spacetime, the conformal extension of Poincaré (or its 2D infinite-dimensional analog Virasoro), the (anti-)de Sitter groups of curved-spacetime cosmological backgrounds, and the diffeomorphism group of general relativity. Each is a Lie group (finite-dimensional in most cases; the 2D conformal case is the famous infinite-dimensional exception) whose representations classify particle content and field content in theories invariant under it. Wigner's 1939 classification of the unitary irreducible representations of the Poincaré group — by mass-squared (a Casimir) and spin/helicity — is the single most consequential application: it defines what a 'particle' is in relativistic quantum field theory.
nodes.domain_of_applicability: Symmetry groups acting globally on a spacetime manifold (or locally, in the case of Diff), used as the symmetry group of a physical theory's action. Excludes: internal gauge groups (classified separately in the SM rep content entry); discrete spacetime symmetries (P, T, C); supersymmetry extensions.
nodes.internal_structure: The major spacetime symmetry groups are organized by which of the following operations they include: spatial rotations (always present); spatial translations (always present); time translations (always present); spatial boosts (Galilean: instantaneous; Lorentz: relativistic); spatial dilations (conformal only); special conformal transformations (conformal only); cosmological-constant-induced curvature (dS/AdS only); arbitrary smooth diffeomorphisms (Diff only). Most of the simple components of these groups are real forms of complex simple Lie algebras of ADE type — see the outgoing cross-classification edge to ade-lie-algebras.

classification_axes.name: symmetry-type
classification_axes.description: The six major spacetime symmetry families. Each label denotes a FAMILY of groups parameterized by spacetime dimension; cell content names the specific group and notes dimensional dependence.

cells.content: Gal(d) = SO(d-1) ⋉ (translations × boosts) ⋉ time-translation; non-relativistic; instantaneous boosts; central extensions (Bargmann) carry physical mass parameter
realized_examples: Newtonian mechanics
non-relativistic quantum mechanics (with Bargmann central extension)
non-relativistic field theories (cold atoms, condensed matter at low temperature)
cells.description: Galilean group: symmetry group of Newtonian spacetime. The central extension (Bargmann 1954) is required for projective representations in QM.

cells.content: ISO(d-1, 1) = SO(d-1, 1) ⋉ ℝ^d; Lorentz group SO(d-1, 1) ≃ Spin(d-1, 1)/Z_2; unitary irreps classified by Wigner 1939
realized_examples: special relativity
relativistic quantum mechanics
the Standard Model (Poincaré-invariant at energies below the Planck scale)
all of relativistic QFT in flat spacetime
cells.description: Poincaré group: symmetry group of Minkowski spacetime. Wigner's 1939 classification of unitary irreps by (mass², spin/helicity) defines the notion of 'particle' in relativistic QFT.

cells.content: For d ≥ 3: SO(d, 2) ≃ Conf(d-1, 1); finite-dimensional. For d = 2: infinite-dimensional Virasoro × Virasoro with central charge c
realized_examples: conformal field theories at critical points
AdS/CFT (the CFT side)
2D worldsheet of string theory (Virasoro constraints)
cells.description: Conformal group: extension of Poincaré by spatial dilations and special conformal transformations. The d = 2 case is qualitatively different — infinite-dimensional Virasoro algebra.

cells.content: SO(d, 1) acting on d-dimensional de Sitter spacetime (positive cosmological constant); finite-dimensional; no positive-energy unitary irreps
realized_examples: our observed accelerating universe (approximately dS in the far future under ΛCDM)
dS-invariant inflation models
the dS/CFT speculative correspondence
cells.description: de Sitter group: isometry group of de Sitter spacetime. The absence of a globally-defined positive-energy generator has major consequences for the swampland conjectures and dS thermodynamics (Gibbons-Hawking).

cells.content: SO(d-1, 2) acting on d-dimensional anti-de Sitter spacetime (negative cosmological constant); positive-energy unitary irreps exist; isomorphic to Conf(d-2, 1) (the AdS/CFT isomorphism at the group level)
realized_examples: AdS_5 × S^5 in Type IIB string theory
AdS/CFT-based holographic models
exact non-perturbative QFT-like theories with negative cc
cells.description: Anti-de Sitter group. The group-level isomorphism SO(d-1,2) ≃ Conf(d-2,1) is the structural origin of AdS/CFT.

cells.content: Diff(M): the full diffeomorphism group of a smooth manifold M; infinite-dimensional; the gauge group of general relativity
realized_examples: general relativity (all observationally-confirmed gravitational physics)
cells.description: General-coordinate (diffeomorphism) group. Infinite-dimensional; gauge of GR. Source of the 'problem of time' in canonical quantum gravity.

closure_status.description: Complete as a classification of the major spacetime symmetry families. Each family's mathematical structure is fully worked out. Does not extend to: (i) which family holds at the Planck scale (QG frontier); (ii) supersymmetric extensions; (iii) whether spacetime symmetry is fundamental or emergent.

predictive_yield.prediction: Existence and classification of particle types in relativistic QFT: each particle is a unitary irrep of Poincaré, labeled by (mass, spin) or (zero mass, helicity). Higher-spin massless particles (s > 2 in 4D) excluded in interacting theories.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Wigner 1939, Ann. Math. 40, 149
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Every observed elementary particle in the SM fits Wigner's classification.

predictive_yield.prediction: Galilean-invariant non-relativistic QM admits projective representations labeled by mass (Bargmann central extension); mass is a conserved superselection sector in NRQM.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Bargmann 1954, Ann. Math. 59, 1
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: The mass superselection rule is a routine feature of NRQM.

predictive_yield.prediction: 2D conformal field theories at criticality have infinite-dimensional Virasoro symmetry; c < 1 minimal models classified by (p, p') labels (ADE / minimal-model classification).
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Belavin, Polyakov, Zamolodchikov 1984, Nucl. Phys. B 241, 333
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: 2D Ising criticality, tricritical Ising, three-state Potts — matched to minimal-model predictions to high precision

related_entries: sm-rep-content
citations: Belavin, Polyakov, Zamolodchikov 1984, Nucl. Phys. B 241, 333
Weinberg 1995, The Quantum Theory of Fields Vol. I, Cambridge

nodes.label: Freed-Hopkins cobordism
nodes.full_name: Freed-Hopkins cobordism classification of invertible topological field theories and symmetry-protected phases
nodes.description: The classification of invertible topological field theories (and the gapped symmetry-protected topological phases they describe) by cobordism. Freed and Hopkins (2016, 2019) proposed that invertible TQFTs in d spacetime dimensions with tangential structure G (specifying orientation, spin, pin, or twisted variants thereof) are classified by [MTG, Σ^{d+1} IZ] — homotopy classes of maps from the Thom spectrum of G to a shift of the Anderson dual of the sphere spectrum. For most cases of physical interest the classification reduces to the torsion subgroup of the (d+1)-dimensional G-bordism group, Ω^G_{d+1}, often combined with its free part. The classification subsumes earlier classifications as special cases: free fermion topological phases (Kitaev's ten-fold way) are recovered for the appropriate tangential structures (Spin, Pin+, Pin-, etc.); bosonic SPT phases match the group-cohomology classification (Chen-Gu-Wen 2011, 2013) in low dimensions; and several Fidkowski-Kitaev-type interaction-induced collapses are computed structurally rather than discovered case-by-case.
nodes.domain_of_applicability: Invertible ("deformation classes of") topological field theories and the SPT phases they describe, for matter with internal symmetries acting via a tangential-structure group G on spacetime. Covers fermionic and bosonic SPTs with discrete internal symmetries and U(1)-valued symmetries; covers crystalline-symmetry-protected phases via twisted tangential structures (a technical refinement). Excludes: non-invertible topological phases (true topological order with anyonic excitations — classified separately by modular tensor categories); gapless topological phases; phases with anomalous boundary states that are not fully captured by the cobordism invariant. Also excludes the cosmological-scale generalization to quantum gravity (anomaly cobordism for gravitational-with-internal-symmetry combinations is studied but the full classification is incomplete).
nodes.internal_structure: Each tangential structure G is a specific Lie group or topological group describing the geometric structure on spacetime: SO is orientation; Spin is the double cover of SO (required for fermions on orientable manifolds); Pin± are unoriented analogs of Spin (relevant when time-reversal symmetry is included); Spin^c is Spin × U(1) / Z_2 (relevant when charge conservation is included). The (d+1)-dimensional G-bordism group Ω^G_{d+1} contains the classification information for invertible phases in d spatial dimensions (or d+1 spacetime dimensions, depending on convention). Famous identities: Ω^{Pin-}_2 = Z_8 (Fidkowski-Kitaev's 1d BDI collapse); Ω^{Pin+}_4 = Z_16 (3+1d time-reversal-invariant fermionic SPT, refining the ten-fold way's class AII Z_2 entry to a Z_16 interacting classification). The classification's structure-group axis is canonically the `group` axis kind in the v14 schema (first use in Phase 2 entries).

classification_axes.name: tangential-structure
classification_axes.description: The five most-studied tangential structures in the Freed-Hopkins classification. SO: orientation (bosonic, oriented). Spin: orientation + spin structure (fermionic, no further symmetry). Pin+ / Pin-: unoriented spin structures (fermionic with time-reversal symmetry; the two signs distinguish T² = -1 from T² = +1). Spin^c: Spin structure twisted by U(1) (fermionic with charge conservation). Additional tangential structures exist (Spin × Z_2^F for fermion-parity-twisted theories; Spin × G for various internal symmetry groups G); these are not enumerated here. Notation follows Freed-Hopkins 2016 §1 and the standard references.
classification_axes.citation: Freed, Hopkins 2016, arXiv:1604.06527 (published 2019, Comm. Math. Phys. 374)

classification_axes.name: spacetime-dimension
classification_axes.description: Spacetime dimension d+1 in which the invertible field theory lives (equivalently: dimension d+1 of the closed manifold whose G-bordism class is the relevant classifying invariant). Conventions in the literature vary on whether the indexed dimension refers to the SPT's spatial dimension d or the field theory's spacetime dimension d+1; this entry uses spacetime dimension d, with cell content giving Ω^G_d for clarity. Cells populated for d ∈ {0, 1, 2, 3, 4}; higher dimensions extend the classification but are less physically relevant and are omitted for compactness.

cells.content: Ω^{SO}_0 = ℤ
cells.description: Oriented points with sign; the integer counts oriented zero-manifolds.
No invertible bosonic SPT phases in 1d.
No invertible bosonic SPT phases in 2d (within the strict-bosonic non-anomalous regime).
No invertible bosonic SPT phases in 3d (without further symmetry refinements).

cells.content: Ω^{SO}_4 = ℤ
cells.description: Classified by the signature of the oriented 4-manifold. The corresponding invertible TQFT is the gravitational theta-term (signature action).

cells.content: Ω^{Spin}_0 = ℤ
Ω^{Spin}_1 = ℤ₂
cells.description: The mod-2 invariant; corresponds to the choice of NS vs R sector in 1d fermionic systems.

cells.content: Ω^{Spin}_2 = ℤ₂
cells.description: Classified by the Arf invariant; the famous 2d Arf-Brown invariant of a quadratic refinement of the intersection form.

cells.content: Ω^{Spin}_3 = 0
Ω^{Spin}_4 = ℤ
cells.description: Classified by the signature (more precisely, by σ/16 — the Â-genus, an integer for Spin manifolds by Rokhlin's theorem).

cells.content: Ω^{Pin+}_0 = ℤ₂
Ω^{Pin+}_2 = ℤ₂
Ω^{Pin+}_3 = ℤ₂
Ω^{Pin+}_4 = ℤ₁₆
cells.description: The famous Z_16 classification of 3+1d topological insulators with time-reversal symmetry T² = -1. Refines the ten-fold-way class AII entry (Z_2 at the free-fermion level) to Z_16 in the interacting case. The Z_16 captures multi-particle interaction effects that the free-fermion classification cannot.
realized_examples: The general 3+1d topological insulator with T² = -1, when interactions are included. The free-fermion case is one of 16 distinct interacting phases.

predictions.prediction: Time-reversal-invariant 3+1d fermionic SPT phases form a Z_16 classification when interactions are included, refining the Z_2 free-fermion classification of the ten-fold way's class AII.
predictions.prediction_citation: Wang, Senthil 2014, Phys. Rev. B 89, 195124; Freed, Hopkins 2016
predictions.confirmation_citation: Theoretical confirmation via multiple independent calculations; experimental confirmation is partial (the higher-Z_16 phases require specific interaction-induced phases not yet definitively isolated experimentally).

cells.content: Ω^{Pin-}_0 = ℤ₂
Ω^{Pin-}_1 = ℤ₂
Ω^{Pin-}_2 = ℤ₈
cells.description: The Fidkowski-Kitaev classification: the Z classification of free fermion class BDI in 1d (ten-fold-way's BDI-d1 cell content) collapses to Z_8 when interactions are included. This is the canonical example of the free-to-interacting reach termination that motivated the topological-phases-classification frontier (added in v14.1).
realized_examples: 1d class BDI topological superconductors with strong interactions — the 8 distinct phases collapse from the free-fermion Z classification.

predictions.prediction: The free-fermion Z classification of 1d class BDI topological superconductors collapses to Z_8 under interactions; equivalently, eight free-fermion BDI phases that are distinct without interactions become continuously deformable into one trivial phase when interactions are turned on.
predictions.prediction_citation: Fidkowski, Kitaev 2010, Phys. Rev. B 81, 134509 (first explicit demonstration); Kitaev 2011 talk on classifications; Freed-Hopkins 2016 (cobordism interpretation)
predictions.confirmation_citation: Theoretical confirmation via multiple independent calculations (Turner-Pollmann-Berg 2011; etc.).

cells.content: Ω^{Spin^c}_0 = ℤ
Ω^{Spin^c}_1 = 0
Ω^{Spin^c}_2 = ℤ
cells.description: Classified by the first Chern number; corresponds to the integer quantum Hall effect's TKNN invariant (Thouless-Kohmoto-Nightingale-den Nijs 1982).
realized_examples: Integer quantum Hall effect (von Klitzing 1980; classified by Chern number)

cells.content: Ω^{Spin^c}_3 = 0
Ω^{Spin^c}_4 = ℤ²
cells.description: Rank-2 free part; classified jointly by the signature and the c_1²/2 invariant (Chern character integrals).

closure_status.description: Partial. The cobordism-classification framework is established and proven; the explicit cobordism groups for the five tangential structures and small dimensions above are computed and standard. What remains partial: (i) higher dimensions and more exotic tangential structures (twisted Spin × G for various G) are not all explicitly computed; (ii) the framework's extension to non-invertible topological phases (true topological order) requires the modular-tensor-category machinery and remains an active research area; (iii) cosmological-scale generalizations (anomaly cobordism for gravity combined with arbitrary symmetry) are partially developed but incomplete. The 'partial' status reflects that the classification is closure-grade for the cases enumerated here but does not extend to the full landscape of SPT and TQFT phases.

predictive_yield.prediction: The free-fermion Z classification of 1d BDI topological superconductors collapses to Z_8 under interactions.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Fidkowski, Kitaev 2010, Phys. Rev. B 81, 134509
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Multiple independent theoretical confirmations (Turner-Pollmann-Berg 2011; Tang-Wen 2012; subsequent literature).

predictive_yield.prediction: Time-reversal-invariant 3+1d fermionic SPT phases with T² = -1 form a Z_16 classification when interactions are included, refining the Z_2 free-fermion classification of class AII.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Wang, Senthil 2014, Phys. Rev. B 89, 195124; Kapustin-Thorngren-Turzillo-Wang 2015, JHEP 12, 052; Freed-Hopkins 2016
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Theoretical: established via multiple frameworks. Experimental: partial — the higher-Z_16 phases require interaction-induced phases not yet isolated experimentally.

predictive_yield.prediction: The integer quantum Hall effect's TKNN invariant (first Chern number) is the Ω^{Spin^c}_2 = Z cobordism invariant; this provides a structural derivation rather than a phenomenological one.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Freed-Hopkins 2016 (structural derivation)
predictive_yield.description: The IQHE was observed in 1980 (von Klitzing); the TKNN invariant interpretation came in 1982. The cobordism-theoretic re-derivation in Freed-Hopkins is retro-explanatory — it provides a uniform framework but did not predict the IQHE.

predictive_yield.prediction: The full classification of interacting fermionic SPT phases for arbitrary tangential structures in arbitrary dimensions reduces to computing cobordism groups Ω^G_{d+1}, a task that the algebraic-topology machinery (Adams spectral sequence, etc.) makes systematically computable.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Freed, Hopkins 2016
predictive_yield.description: Methodological prediction. Not unconfirmed in the sense of being challenged, but the prediction is about a research program's reach; many cases remain to be computed even where the framework is accepted as correct.
