nodes.label: Modular tensor categories
nodes.full_name: Classification of modular tensor categories (anyonic topological order, rational 2D CFT data, Reshetikhin-Turaev TQFTs)
nodes.description: A modular tensor category (MTC) is a unitary braided fusion category whose modular S-matrix is non-degenerate. MTCs classify three apparently distinct objects: (i) the algebraic data of rational 2D conformal field theories (Moore-Seiberg axioms, 1989); (ii) the anyonic content of (2+1)D topologically-ordered ground states (Kitaev, Wen, Levin, Wang); (iii) the input data for Reshetikhin-Turaev TQFTs in 3D (Reshetikhin-Turaev 1991). These three identifications are theorems, not analogies — Moore-Seiberg established the rational-CFT correspondence, the topological-order correspondence is the consensus framing of intrinsic topological order, and the RT-TQFT construction gives the explicit 3D quantum-invariant. The classification proceeds by rank (number of simple objects / anyon types). Rowell-Stong-Wang (2009) gave the complete classification of unitary MTCs up to rank ≤ 5; partial results at higher rank exist (notably the Drinfeld center construction for double of fusion categories) but a complete classification at general rank remains open. The full data of an MTC are: a finite set of simple objects, associative fusion rules with Frobenius reciprocity, F-symbols satisfying the pentagon axiom, R-symbols satisfying the hexagon axiom, and a modular S-matrix that is non-degenerate. Two MTCs are equivalent iff their fusion-and-braiding data agree up to natural isomorphism of categories.
nodes.domain_of_applicability: Anyonic content of (2+1)D topologically-ordered quantum phases; chiral algebra data of rational 2D conformal field theories; algebraic data of 3D Reshetikhin-Turaev topological quantum field theories. Excludes: SPT phases without intrinsic topological order (these are classified by group cohomology / ten-fold-way / cobordism, not MTC); irrational 2D CFTs (continuous Virasoro modules — bootstrap territory).
nodes.organizing_principle: A finite collection of simple objects (anyon types) with associative tensor product (fusion rules), unitary braiding (R-symbols, equivalently a braided structure on the category), and modular S/T transformation matrices satisfying the modular axioms (modular invariance of the partition function on the torus). The S-matrix non-degeneracy distinguishes MTCs (modular = non-degenerate braiding) from more general braided fusion categories. Two anyon types differ iff at least one of (fusion rule, F-symbol, R-symbol, modular S-row) differs.

classification_axes.name: rank
classification_axes.description: Number of simple objects (distinct anyon types), including the vacuum (which is always present as the monoidal unit). Rank-1 has only the vacuum; rank-2 includes Fibonacci, semion; rank-3 includes Ising; rank-4 includes toric code, three-fermion; etc.
classification_axes.citation: Rowell, Stong, Wang 2009, Commun. Math. Phys. 292, 343 (classification at low rank)

classification_axes.name: mtc-family
classification_axes.description: Family label for canonical MTC examples. SU(2)_k denotes the WZW-derived SU(2) level-k family (rank k+1, central charge c = 3k/(k+2)); these are also the ADE-modular-invariant MTCs at the A-series. Toric-code is the canonical Z_2 gauge theory MTC. Three-fermion (3F) is the rank-4 chiral fermionic MTC with c=4. Doubled-Ising is the Z_2 orbifold of Ising × Ising-bar. Drinfeld-center is the construction Z(C) producing a non-chiral MTC from any fusion category C. Exceptional covers MTCs not in the named series (e.g., Haagerup-related).

cells.content: Trivial MTC: only the vacuum object 1. Fusion rules: 1 ⊗ 1 = 1. S-matrix is the 1×1 identity. Central charge c = 0.
realized_examples: Trivial gapped phase (no topological order)
Vec (the category of complex vector spaces, as a trivial braided fusion category)
cells.description: The unique rank-1 MTC. Every fusion category has the trivial MTC as a sub-category. Realized physically as the gapped trivial phase (a product state) and as the boundary of a (2+1)D trivial phase. All other rank-1 cells are structurally-excluded by the axes' design (rank=1 forces only the vacuum family).
citations: Etingof, Gelaki, Nikshych, Ostrik 2015, AMS Tensor Categories monograph

cells.content: Semion MTC: objects {1, s} with s ⊗ s = 1 and braiding eigenvalue i (the semion's exchange phase is i, hence 'half a fermion'). Chiral central charge c = 1 (mod 8 for bosonic structure). Modular S = (1/√2) [[1,1],[1,-1]].
realized_examples: U(1)_2 Chern-Simons theory
Kalmeyer-Laughlin chiral spin liquid (proposed for triangular antiferromagnets)
Boundary of (2+1)D bosonic IQHE-like phase with σ_xy = 2 (in units of e²/h)
cells.description: The simplest non-trivial chiral MTC. Semions are abelian anyons with statistical phase π/2 (half the fermion phase π). The chirality is captured by c = 1 ≠ 0; the semion MTC has no time-reversal-invariant realization. Conjectured but not definitively observed in materials; Kalmeyer-Laughlin's proposed chiral spin liquid is the canonical candidate.
citations: Wen 1991, Phys. Rev. B 44, 2664 (Z_2 and U(1) Chern-Simons theories)

cells.content: Fibonacci MTC: objects {1, τ} with τ ⊗ τ = 1 ⊕ τ (the Fibonacci fusion rule). Quantum dimension d_τ = φ = (1+√5)/2 (the golden ratio). Central charge c = 14/5. Non-abelian — τ is a non-abelian anyon.
realized_examples: (G_2)_1 Chern-Simons theory
Yang-Lee non-unitary CFT (after a unitarization)
Predicted in certain fractional Chern insulators (no confirmed laboratory realization as of 2026)
cells.description: The simplest non-abelian MTC. Fibonacci anyons are universal for topological quantum computation (Freedman-Kitaev-Larsen-Wang 2003): braiding alone generates a dense subgroup of the unitary group on the topological Hilbert space. Searches for Fibonacci anyons in fractional Chern insulators and certain ν = 12/5 FQHE states are ongoing.
citations: Freedman, Larsen, Wang 2002, Commun. Math. Phys. 227, 605 (universal quantum computation by topological methods)
Trebst, Troyer, Wang, Ludwig 2008, Prog. Theor. Phys. Suppl. 176, 384 (review of Fibonacci-anyon computation)

cells.content: Lee-Yang MTC: non-unitary; objects {1, τ_LY} with τ_LY ⊗ τ_LY = 1 ⊕ τ_LY. Same fusion rule as Fibonacci but negative quantum dimension d_{τ_LY} = -1/φ < 0. Central charge c = -22/5.
realized_examples: Lee-Yang edge singularity in dilute Ising at imaginary magnetic field
Minimal model M(2,5) (non-unitary)
cells.description: The canonical non-unitary MTC. Negative quantum dimension excludes physical realization in unitary quantum systems. Important as an algebraic example demonstrating that non-unitary MTCs exist and have the same fusion-rule data as unitary MTCs (Fibonacci) while differing in braiding signs.
citations: Mussardo 1992, Phys. Rep. 218, 215 (review of non-unitary CFT)

cells.content: Ising MTC: objects {1, σ, ψ} with fusion σ ⊗ σ = 1 ⊕ ψ, σ ⊗ ψ = σ, ψ ⊗ ψ = 1. Quantum dimensions: d_1 = d_ψ = 1, d_σ = √2. Central charge c = 1/2 (mod 8). Non-abelian — σ is a non-abelian anyon (Majorana-like).
realized_examples: Majorana zero modes at vortex cores of (p+ip) superconductors
Boundary of (2+1)D Kitaev's honeycomb model in the gapped non-abelian (B) phase
Predicted (with partial confirmation) in ν=5/2 FQHE Moore-Read Pfaffian state
cells.description: The Ising MTC is the canonical non-abelian Majorana-anyon system. The σ anyon supports non-abelian braiding via the Majorana zero mode it hosts. Braiding alone is NOT universal for quantum computation (gives only the Clifford group); a non-topological gate is needed to achieve universality. Major experimental relevance: Moore-Read Pfaffian in ν=5/2 FQHE (Willett interferometry experiments and Banerjee thermal-Hall measurements give partial evidence; competing PH-Pfaffian state predicts opposite chirality and remains contested).
citations: Moore, Read 1991, Nucl. Phys. B 360, 362 (Pfaffian state for ν=5/2)
Kitaev 2006, Ann. Phys. 321, 2 (anyons in an exactly solved model — honeycomb)
Nayak, Simon, Stern, Freedman, Das Sarma 2008, Rev. Mod. Phys. 80, 1083 (review)

cells.content: SU(2)_2 Chern-Simons MTC: objects labeled by isospin j ∈ {0, 1/2, 1}. Central charge c = 3·2/(2+2) = 3/2. Distinct from Ising despite both being rank-3 with σ-like anyon — SU(2)_2 has different chiral central charge and different F-symbols.
realized_examples: SU(2)_2 WZW model
Effective theory of a chiral spin liquid with SU(2) global symmetry
cells.description: Rank-3 SU(2)_k MTC at k=2. The j=1/2 anyon is non-abelian with quantum dimension √2 (similar to Ising's σ), but the chiral central charge c=3/2 differs from Ising's c=1/2. This pair (Ising, SU(2)_2) is the canonical example showing that rank does not uniquely determine MTC — two distinct unitary MTCs share rank 3 but differ in chiral central charge.
citations: Witten 1989, Commun. Math. Phys. 121, 351 (Chern-Simons gauge theory and Jones polynomial)

cells.content: Tricritical Ising MTC (within rank-3 unitary classification): central charge c = 7/10. Three primaries with conformal weights (0, 1/10, 6/10) in the unitary M(4,5) minimal model.
realized_examples: Tricritical Ising phase transition in dilute Ising magnet
Boundary of supersymmetric (2+1)D topological superconductor
cells.description: The tricritical Ising MTC is another rank-3 unitary MTC distinct from both Ising and SU(2)_2. Its primaries arise from the M(4,5) Virasoro minimal model. Demonstrates the rich rank-3 unitary MTC zoo classified by Rowell-Stong-Wang.
citations: Friedan, Qiu, Shenker 1984, Phys. Rev. Lett. 52, 1575 (unitary minimal models)

cells.content: Toric code MTC (Z_2 gauge theory): objects {1, e, m, ε} where ε = e⊗m. Fusion: e⊗e = m⊗m = ε⊗ε = 1; e⊗m = ε. Quantum dimensions all 1 (abelian). Chiral central charge c = 0 — non-chiral; time-reversal-invariant.
realized_examples: Kitaev's toric code on a square lattice (experimentally realized in superconducting qubits — Google 2023, IBM 2024 — and in neutral-atom arrays — Lukin group 2021)
Z_2 spin liquids (candidate materials: certain pyrochlores; α-RuCl_3 in narrow regime)
Boundary of (2+1)D Z_2 SPT in the bosonic case
cells.description: The toric code is the canonical Z_2 gauge theory MTC, foundational to topological quantum error correction. Its abelian Z_2×Z_2 anyon content provides the simplest topological code with finite code distance. Experimental realization in quantum-computing hardware is the most concrete confirmed prediction of MTC theory: digital simulation has produced anyonic excitations and braiding signatures consistent with toric-code MTC predictions. Spin-liquid candidates in α-RuCl_3 remain contested as of 2026.
citations: Kitaev 2003, Ann. Phys. 303, 2 (fault-tolerant quantum computation by anyons)
Satzinger et al. (Google Quantum AI) 2021, Science 374, 1237 (realizing topologically ordered states on a quantum processor)
Banerjee et al. 2017, Nat. Mater. 17, 4 (Z_2 spin liquid in α-RuCl_3 — contested)

cells.content: Three-fermion (3F) MTC: objects {1, ψ_1, ψ_2, ψ_3} all fermionic (semion-squared exchange phases). Fusion: ψ_i ⊗ ψ_j = ψ_k for i ≠ j ≠ k, ψ_i ⊗ ψ_i = 1. Central charge c = 4 (mod 8). Chiral, no time-reversal-invariant realization.
realized_examples: Predicted boundary of certain (2+1)D fermionic SPT with three-band structure (no confirmed material realization as of 2026)
U(1)_4 × U(1)_{-4} chiral phase with appropriate K-matrix
cells.description: The 3F MTC is the canonical example of a non-abelian-looking rank-4 abelian MTC. All three non-trivial anyons are fermions; their pairwise fusion is non-trivial. Important as a structural example bridging the toric-code (fully abelian non-chiral) and Ising (non-abelian chiral) rank-domains; 3F is abelian chiral.
citations: Kitaev 2006, Ann. Phys. 321, 2 (3F in the honeycomb model spectrum)
Lan, Kong, Wen 2017, Phys. Rev. B 95, 235140 (classification of (2+1)D topological orders)

cells.content: Doubled Ising MTC: Z(Ising) = Drinfeld center of the Ising fusion category. Rank 9, but at restricted topological sectors reduces to rank 4 in some constructions. Actually doubled-Ising in its canonical (rank-9) form: objects (a, b̄) where a, b ∈ {1, σ, ψ}. Non-chiral: c = 0. Time-reversal-invariant.
realized_examples: Levin-Wen string-net model with Ising fusion data
Boundary of (3+1)D phase with non-abelian intrinsic topological order on the boundary
cells.description: Note: doubled-Ising MTC is rank 9 (3×3 pairs of (anyon, anti-anyon)) in its full form. The cell here represents the doubled-Ising construction at restricted-rank truncations relevant to specific physical realizations. The full Drinfeld center Z(Ising) is the canonical example of a non-chiral (c=0) MTC realizing non-abelian anyon physics. Realized in Levin-Wen string-net constructions; structurally distinct from chiral Ising in that braiding alone is universal for non-Clifford gates in the doubled context.
citations: Levin, Wen 2005, Phys. Rev. B 71, 045110 (string-net condensation)
Kong, Wen 2014, arXiv:1405.5858 (braided fusion categories of topological order)

cells.content: SU(2)_3 Chern-Simons MTC: objects labeled by isospin j ∈ {0, 1/2, 1, 3/2}. Central charge c = 3·3/(3+2) = 9/5. The j=1/2 and j=3/2 anyons are non-abelian; j=1 is abelian (the Z_2 grading). Equivalent to a Fibonacci-extended construction.
realized_examples: Read-Rezayi parafermion states (k=3 case) in FQHE
Proposed at ν = 12/5 FQHE plateau
SU(2)_3 WZW model
cells.description: Rank-4 SU(2)_k MTC at k=3. Contains a Fibonacci sub-MTC (the integer-spin sector {j=0, j=1}). The non-abelian j=3/2 anyon supports universal topological quantum computation (universality inherited from Fibonacci sub-content). Read-Rezayi state at k=3 is the canonical FQHE realization candidate; ν=12/5 plateau is the prime experimental target but evidence as of 2026 is partial.
citations: Read, Rezayi 1999, Phys. Rev. B 59, 8084 (parafermion FQHE states)
Xia et al. 2004, Phys. Rev. Lett. 93, 176809 (observation of ν=12/5 plateau)

cells.content: SU(2)_4 Chern-Simons MTC: objects labeled by j ∈ {0, 1/2, 1, 3/2, 2}. Central charge c = 3·4/(4+2) = 2.
realized_examples: SU(2)_4 WZW model
Proposed in higher-Landau-level FQHE constructions
cells.description: SU(2)_k MTC at k=4. Contains the doubled-Ising and three-fermion MTCs as truncations. Continues the SU(2)_k family pattern (rank-(k+1), central-charge 3k/(k+2)) into rank 5 and beyond.
citations: Bakalov, Kirillov 2001, Lectures on Tensor Categories and Modular Functor (AMS) — SU(2)_k tables

cells.content: Fibonacci × Fibonacci-bar MTC (Drinfeld-center-like construction): rank 4 actually (1, τ, τ̄, τ⊗τ̄). Or alternatively a rank-5 exceptional from doubled SO(3)_3 truncation. Central charge c = 0 (non-chiral, if doubled construction).
realized_examples: Drinfeld center Z(Fib) of Fibonacci fusion category
Levin-Wen string-net with Fibonacci input
cells.description: A canonical doubled-Fibonacci construction: non-chiral MTC of rank 4-5 (depending on construction) used in non-abelian topological code proposals. Important for topological quantum computation: gives universal gate set via braiding alone in the doubled (non-chiral) context, unlike chiral Fibonacci which is universal in the chiral context.
citations: Levin, Wen 2005, Phys. Rev. B 71, 045110 (string-net construction)
Bonderson 2007, PhD thesis Caltech (non-abelian anyons and topological quantum computation)

cells.content: SU(2)_5 Chern-Simons MTC: rank 6, j ∈ {0, 1/2, 1, 3/2, 2, 5/2}. Central charge c = 3·5/(5+2) = 15/7.
realized_examples: SU(2)_5 WZW model
cells.description: Continuation of the SU(2)_k family at k=5. Higher-rank SU(2)_k MTCs have not been the focus of experimental searches but are well-classified mathematically. Notable: SU(2)_k for k → ∞ approaches the irrational free-boson regime, which exits MTC into general bootstrap-allowed CFT territory.

cells.content: structurally-excluded
cells.description: Structurally excluded: rank 1 uniquely fixes mtc-family = vacuum (the only rank-1 MTC is the trivial one). All non-vacuum families require rank ≥ 2.

cells.content: structurally-excluded
cells.description: Structurally excluded: toric code is intrinsically rank-4 (vacuum + e + m + ε). No rank-2 MTC has the toric-code fusion structure.

cells.content: structurally-excluded
cells.description: Structurally excluded: toric-code rank is 4, not 3.

cells.content: structurally-excluded
cells.description: Structurally excluded: three-fermion MTC is intrinsically rank 4 (vacuum + 3 fermions).

closure_status.description: Partial. The classification framework (unitary braided fusion categories with non-degenerate modular S-matrix) is established and rigorously defined. Complete classification at rank ≤ 5 is established (Rowell-Stong-Wang 2009 plus subsequent refinements). At rank ≥ 6 the classification is partial: SU(2)_k and other Lie-group Chern-Simons families are well-understood, the Drinfeld center construction Z(C) gives non-chiral MTCs from any fusion category, but exhaustive lists at general rank are open. Non-unitary MTCs and modular categories over non-trivially-graded contexts (super-MTCs, fermionic MTCs) are partial classifications with active mathematical work. The closure-status `partial` reflects this: well-classified at low rank, framework-complete but list-incomplete at high rank.

predictive_yield.description: Theoretical prediction (Freedman-Larsen-Wang 2002): the braid group representation on the Fibonacci MTC's topological Hilbert space generates a dense subgroup of the unitary group, hence braiding alone universal. No laboratory realization of Fibonacci anyons confirmed as of 2026; searches continue in fractional Chern insulators and certain FQHE states (notably ν=12/5).
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Freedman, Larsen, Wang 2002, Commun. Math. Phys. 227, 605
predictive_yield.prediction: Fibonacci anyons support universal topological quantum computation via braiding alone.

predictive_yield.description: Multiple platforms (superconducting qubits — Google Quantum AI 2021; neutral atoms — Harvard/MIT 2021; trapped ions — Quantinuum 2023) have prepared toric-code ground states and demonstrated anyonic excitations consistent with MTC predictions. The most stringent test — explicit braiding of e and m anyons to confirm mutual statistics — was achieved by Satzinger et al. (Google Quantum AI 2021) and refined in subsequent work.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Kitaev 2003, Ann. Phys. 303, 2
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Satzinger et al. (Google Quantum AI) 2021, Science 374, 1237; Bluvstein et al. (Harvard) 2022, Nature 604, 451; Iqbal et al. (Quantinuum) 2023, Nature 626, 505
predictive_yield.prediction: Toric code Z_2 anyons (e, m, ε) realized in digital quantum simulation with measurable braiding signatures.

predictive_yield.description: Moore-Read 1991 prediction: the ν=5/2 FQHE plateau hosts Ising-MTC anyons (Pfaffian or anti-Pfaffian state). Experimental status 2026: Willett interferometry experiments (2009-2013) and Banerjee thermal-Hall measurements (2018) provide partial evidence consistent with non-abelian Ising statistics. The PH-Pfaffian state (Son 2015) gives opposite chiral central charge and is favored by some thermal-conductance experiments. As of 2026 the correct topological state remains contested; the question is open whether Pfaffian, anti-Pfaffian, or PH-Pfaffian is realized.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Moore, Read 1991, Nucl. Phys. B 360, 362
predictive_yield.prediction: Non-abelian Ising anyons realized in ν=5/2 FQHE with Pfaffian topological order.
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Willett, Pfeiffer, West 2010, Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. 107, 8853 (interferometry evidence); Banerjee et al. 2018, Nature 559, 205 (thermal Hall conductance, half-quantized — partial confirmation, contested by PH-Pfaffian interpretation)

predictive_yield.description: Banerjee et al. (2017) reported half-quantized thermal Hall conductance in α-RuCl_3 under in-plane magnetic field, interpreted as evidence for Kitaev-honeycomb-model-like non-abelian spin liquid. Subsequent re-measurements (e.g., Czajka et al. 2021) showed the half-quantization is not universal across samples and may be phonon-thermal-Hall not topological. As of 2026 the spin-liquid identification in α-RuCl_3 is contested.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Kitaev 2006, Ann. Phys. 321, 2
predictive_yield.prediction: Z_2 spin liquid topological order (toric-code MTC at low temperatures) in α-RuCl_3.
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Banerjee et al. 2017, Nat. Mater. 17, 4 (initial evidence); Czajka et al. 2021, Nat. Phys. 17, 915 (counter-evidence: non-universality, phonon contribution) — observations contested

predictive_yield.description: Read-Rezayi 1999 prediction: the ν=12/5 FQHE plateau hosts SU(2)_3-like MTC. Observed plateau (Xia et al. 2004) but no detailed topological characterization. Universal topological quantum computation accessible via braiding if the prediction holds.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Read, Rezayi 1999, Phys. Rev. B 59, 8084
predictive_yield.prediction: Read-Rezayi parafermion state at ν=12/5 FQHE.

predictive_yield.description: Moore-Seiberg 1989 established the categorical-equivalence between rational 2D CFTs and MTCs. This is structurally a classification theorem about a pre-existing object (rational CFT, established since the 1970s minimal-model era) rather than a prediction of new phenomena. The MTC framework reorganizes and clarifies rational CFT structure but does not predict new rational CFTs to be observed. Retro-explanatory.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Moore, Seiberg 1989, Commun. Math. Phys. 123, 177
predictive_yield.prediction: Moore-Seiberg classification: every rational 2D CFT has the algebraic data of an MTC.

predictive_yield.description: Reshetikhin-Turaev 1991 construction produces a 3D TQFT from any MTC, yielding quantum invariants of 3-manifolds and links (the Jones polynomial generalizes to all MTCs). This is a mathematical confirmation rather than a physical experiment, but it's rigorously established — every MTC produces well-defined 3-manifold and link invariants, computable in polynomial time.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Reshetikhin, Turaev 1991, Invent. Math. 103, 547
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Bakalov, Kirillov 2001 (textbook proof)
predictive_yield.prediction: Reshetikhin-Turaev TQFTs as 3-manifold invariants from MTC data.

related_entries: cft-bootstrap-exclusion-regions (MTC is the rational sub-case)
ade-modular-invariants (ADE-modular is the SU(2)_k subfamily of MTC)
freed-hopkins-cobordism (invertible-MTC sub-case overlaps cobordism classification)
tenfold-way (MTC classifies intrinsic topological order; ten-fold way classifies SPT phases; the two are complementary in the full topological-phase classification)
citations: Moore, Seiberg 1989, Commun. Math. Phys. 123, 177 (rational CFT axioms)
Reshetikhin, Turaev 1991, Invent. Math. 103, 547 (RT-TQFT from MTC)
Kitaev 2006, Ann. Phys. 321, 2 (anyons in an exactly solved model)
Rowell, Stong, Wang 2009, Commun. Math. Phys. 292, 343 (classification at low rank)
Etingof, Gelaki, Nikshych, Ostrik 2015, AMS Tensor Categories monograph
Lan, Kong, Wen 2017, Phys. Rev. B 95, 235140 (full classification of 2+1D topological orders, low rank)

nodes.label: Generalized symmetries / SymTFT
nodes.full_name: Classification of generalized symmetries via Symmetry Topological Field Theory (SymTFT)
nodes.description: The generalized-symmetry / SymTFT classification organizes the symmetry data of a quantum field theory across an extended notion of 'symmetry' that includes: (i) ordinary (0-form) group symmetries acting on local operators; (ii) higher-form (p-form) symmetries acting on p-dimensional extended operators or defects; (iii) non-invertible / categorical symmetries with fusion-category structure (no group inverse); (iv) 2-group and higher-group structures with mixed extensions. The unifying framework: any d-dim QFT with symmetry data S has an associated (d+1)-dim topological field theory — the SymTFT — on the bulk; the QFT lives on the boundary. The bulk SymTFT is invertible (anomaly-inflow) and cobordism-classified. The boundary's symmetry anomalies are anomalies of the bulk TFT. This framework, developed 2014-2024 (Gaiotto-Kapustin-Seiberg-Willett 2015; Freed-Moore-Teleman 2022; Apruzzi et al. 2023), unifies anomaly analysis across the symmetry classes by reducing them to the cobordism classification of the bulk invertible TFT.
nodes.domain_of_applicability: Symmetry data of any QFT (CFT, gauge theory, sigma model, ...). Covers ordinary continuous and discrete symmetries, higher-form symmetries acting on Wilson/'t Hooft lines and surfaces, non-invertible / categorical symmetries from gauging and from anomalies, and 2-group / higher-group structures arising from mixed extensions. Excludes: spacetime symmetries qua spacetime (these live in the separate spacetime-symmetry-groups classification — generalized-symmetries handles INTERNAL symmetry data; spacetime symmetries appear as boundary conditions or as the 'frame' on which the SymTFT bulk lives).
nodes.organizing_principle: A d-dim QFT's symmetry data is captured by the topological data of its (d+1)-dim SymTFT bulk. Two QFTs have the same symmetry structure iff their SymTFTs have the same topological data (cobordism class, defect-line content, fusion structure of the bulk operator algebra). Form-degree p organizes how the symmetry acts: 0-form on local operators, 1-form on line operators, ..., p-form on p-dimensional defects. Structure-type distinguishes invertible (group-like) from non-invertible (categorical) from 2-group (mixed extension).

classification_axes.name: spacetime-dim
classification_axes.description: Dimension d of the QFT spacetime where the symmetry acts. The SymTFT bulk lives in d+1 dimensions. Phenomenologically-relevant cases: d=4 (QFTs of nature); d=3 (condensed-matter (2+1)D effective theories, topological order); d=2 (CFTs, lattice models, holography); d=5,6 (string-theoretic / M-theory).

classification_axes.name: form-degree
classification_axes.description: Form-degree p of the symmetry. A p-form symmetry acts on p-dimensional extended operators / defects in the QFT. 0-form acts on local operators; 1-form on line operators (Wilson/'t Hooft lines); 2-form on surface operators; etc. Structurally constrained: p ≤ d-1 for any non-trivial action on operators with positive codimension. Cells with p ≥ d are structurally-excluded.

classification_axes.name: structure-type
classification_axes.description: Algebraic structure of the symmetry. discrete-invertible-group: finite group acting invertibly (e.g., Z_n). continuous-invertible-Lie-group: Lie group acting invertibly (e.g., U(1), SU(N)). non-invertible-Tambara-Yamagami: Z_n × Z_n duality fusion category, the simplest non-invertible class (Kramers-Wannier-type). non-invertible-categorical-general: arbitrary fusion-category-valued symmetries. 2-group-mixed: a 2-group with non-trivial Postnikov class mixing 0-form and 1-form. higher-group: 3-group and beyond.

cells.content: 2D 0-form discrete symmetry. Canonical examples: Z_2 of Ising CFT, S_3 of 3-state Potts, Z_n of Z_n parafermions. Anomaly structure: H^3(G, U(1)) ≃ Ω_3^G classification.
realized_examples: Ising CFT Z_2 spin-flip symmetry (anomaly-free)
Tricritical Ising Z_2 × Z_2 (with mixed anomaly)
3-state Potts S_3 symmetry
cells.description: The foundational 0-form discrete case in 2D. SymTFT bulk is 3D Dijkgraaf-Witten gauge theory with group G; the boundary is the symmetric 2D CFT. Anomalies are 3-cocycles in H^3(G, U(1)) ≃ cobordism Ω_3^{SO×BG}_{spin}.

cells.content: 2D 0-form non-invertible Tambara-Yamagami symmetry. Kramers-Wannier-duality-like fusion: D × D = 1 + Z + ... (D = duality defect, Z = group elements). The fusion is non-invertible: D² is NOT 1.
realized_examples: Kramers-Wannier duality of 2D Ising (D acts as the self-duality)
Z_n × Z_n duality category for the rank-2 toric-code-like phase
Verlinde-line dualities in c=1 free-boson CFT
cells.description: The canonical first example of non-invertible symmetry in 2D, predating SymTFT formalism (Tambara-Yamagami 1998). The SymTFT bulk is a 3D Reshetikhin-Turaev TQFT with non-trivial fusion category data — the same data as a (rank-2 or higher) MTC.
citations: Bhardwaj, Tachikawa 2017, JHEP 03, 189 (finite symmetries and category theory)
Aasen, Mong, Fendley 2016, J. Phys. A 49, 354001 (Kramers-Wannier as topological defect)

cells.content: 2D 0-form general fusion-category symmetry (Verlinde lines and beyond). Topological defect lines (TDLs) in 2D CFT carry fusion-category structure C; non-invertible TDLs occur whenever C is not pointed.
realized_examples: Verlinde lines of 2D rational CFTs (Petkova-Zuber 2001)
Topological defect lines in tricritical Ising minimal model (Frolich-Fuchs-Runkel-Schweigert 2007)
Non-invertible defects in c=1 free-boson at irrational radius
cells.description: The broad class of categorical symmetries in 2D, of which Tambara-Yamagami is a sub-case. SymTFT bulk is a 3D Turaev-Viro / Reshetikhin-Turaev TQFT specified by the input fusion category. Closely related to MTC (the MTC IS the SymTFT bulk for the chiral case).
citations: Frohlich, Fuchs, Runkel, Schweigert 2007, Nucl. Phys. B 763, 354
Chang, Lin, Shao, Wang, Yin 2019, JHEP 01, 026 (topological defect lines and renormalization group flows)

cells.content: 3D 0-form discrete symmetry. Examples: Z_2 of 3D Ising CFT at criticality, time-reversal Z_2^T of (2+1)D topological insulators in the AII class.
realized_examples: 3D Ising critical point Z_2 (above the Wilson-Fisher fixed point)
Z_2 time-reversal of topological insulators (AII symmetry class)
Z_4 chiral symmetry of certain (2+1)D Dirac fermions
cells.description: 3D 0-form discrete symmetries with anomaly classification H^4(G, U(1)) ≃ Ω_4^{SO × BG}. Drives the SPT (symmetry-protected topological) phase classification in (2+1)D, partially overlapping with the ten-fold way for fermionic phases.
citations: Wen 2014, Phys. Rev. B 89, 035147 (SPT phases via group cohomology)
Kapustin 2014, arXiv:1403.1467 (cobordism and bosonic SPT phases)

cells.content: 3D 1-form discrete symmetry acting on line operators. Canonical case: Z_N 1-form symmetry of U(1)_N Chern-Simons theory (acts on Wilson lines).
realized_examples: Z_2 × Z_2 1-form of 3D Z_2 toric code (the e-line and m-line are 1-form symmetry charges)
Z_N 1-form of U(1)_N Chern-Simons theory
Z_N 1-form of Z_N gauge theory in (2+1)D
cells.description: The 1-form symmetry generators are surfaces in 3D bulk (codimension 2 in the bulk SymTFT). For Z_N toric code in 3D, the e and m anyons together generate Z_N × Z_N 1-form symmetry with mutual braiding 2π/N — a mixed anomaly.
citations: Gaiotto, Kapustin, Seiberg, Willett 2015, JHEP 02, 172 (generalized global symmetries)

cells.content: 4D 0-form continuous Lie-group symmetry. Examples: SU(N_f) chiral flavor symmetry of N_f-flavor QCD; U(1)_B baryon number; U(1)_em electromagnetic gauge (when treated as global at the boundary).
realized_examples: SU(3)_L × SU(3)_R chiral flavor of 3-flavor QCD
SU(N) flavor symmetry of large-N QCD
U(1)_B baryon number in SM
SU(2) custodial symmetry of SM electroweak sector
cells.description: Continuous Lie-group global symmetries with anomaly structure encoded in H^4(BG, ℝ) or in continuous cobordism. The chiral flavor symmetry of QCD has the canonical ABJ anomaly under U(1)_A. SymTFT bulk is a 5D 'theta-term-like' invertible TFT determined by the Pontryagin class data.
citations: 't Hooft 1976, Phys. Rev. Lett. 37, 8 (chiral anomaly and instantons)
Witten 1983, Nucl. Phys. B 223, 422 (current algebra anomaly)

cells.content: 4D 1-form center symmetry of pure SU(N) Yang-Mills. The Z_N center acts on Wilson lines, classifying them by N-ality. Spontaneously broken in deconfined (high-T) phase; unbroken in confined (low-T) phase. Order parameter: Polyakov loop.
realized_examples: Z_N 1-form of pure SU(N) YM in 4D (canonical Komargodski-Sulejmanpasic-Unsal example)
Z_2 magnetic 1-form of free 4D Maxwell theory ('t Hooft line)
Z_n 1-form of 4D Z_n gauge theory
cells.description: The 1-form center symmetry organizes the confinement-deconfinement phase transition of pure YM as a symmetry-breaking transition. With 't Hooft anomaly matching (Komargodski-Sulejmanpasic-Unsal 2017), one can rule out trivially-gapped phases in certain regions — a structural constraint coming purely from the generalized-symmetry analysis. Established and used in 4D lattice gauge theory phase-diagram analysis.
citations: 't Hooft 1978, Nucl. Phys. B 138, 1 (Z_N center symmetry)
Komargodski, Sulejmanpasic, Unsal 2017, Phys. Rev. B 97, 054418 (walls and bound states)

cells.content: 4D 0-form non-invertible symmetry from ABJ anomaly. The chiral U(1)_A of QED is anomalous and naively breaks; recast as a NON-INVERTIBLE discrete Z-valued (or rational) symmetry by gauging instanton number. Choi-Cordova-Hsin-Lam-Shao 2022; Cordova-Ohmori 2022.
realized_examples: U(1)_A of massless QED — recast as a fractional / non-invertible symmetry
Chiral symmetry of massless QCD — partial non-invertible structure
Theta-shifts in 4D N=4 SYM viewed as non-invertible symmetry
cells.description: A major recent (2022) discovery: anomalous continuous symmetries that would naively be lost can be reorganized as non-invertible discrete symmetries. This converts the 'spoiled symmetry' picture into a 'transformed symmetry' picture, with concrete operational consequences (selection rules from the non-invertible structure).
citations: Choi, Cordova, Hsin, Lam, Shao 2022, Phys. Rev. Lett. 129, 161601
Cordova, Ohmori 2022, Phys. Rev. X 13, 011034 (non-invertible chiral symmetry)

cells.content: 4D 2-group structure mixing 0-form and 1-form symmetries via Postnikov class. Canonical example: axion-photon system where the axion shift symmetry (0-form U(1)) mixes with the photon 1-form Z by anomaly inflow.
realized_examples: Axion-photon system (the canonical 4D 2-group)
Theta-term-shift mixing with electromagnetic 1-form in 4D
Cordova-Dumitrescu-Intriligator 2-group of 4D N=2 theories
cells.description: 2-groups arise when 0-form and 1-form symmetries mix non-trivially via a Postnikov class — the 0-form symmetry, when acted on, deforms the 1-form symmetry's anomaly. This is genuinely 'higher-symmetry' content; not reducible to direct-product structure.
citations: Tachikawa 2020, SciPost Phys. 8, 015 (on gauging finite subgroups)

cells.content: 4D 2-form discrete symmetry acting on 2D defects. Generators are codimension-3 in 4D = 1D in bulk SymTFT. Examples: 2-form of free 2-form gauge theory in 4D; emergent 2-form in certain (3+1)D phases.
realized_examples: Free 2-form gauge theory in 4D (Bantay-Vafa-like models)
Emergent 2-form symmetry in (3+1)D Z_2 topological order
cells.description: 2-form symmetries in 4D act on surface operators (2D defects). Drives the classification of higher-form gauge theories and certain (3+1)D topological orders. Less common in phenomenologically-relevant models.
citations: Gaiotto, Kapustin, Seiberg, Willett 2015 (treatment of higher-form generally)

cells.content: 6D 2-form center symmetry of the (2,0) theory. The 6D N=(2,0) SCFT has 2-form symmetry corresponding to the center of the gauge group (A_n, D_n, E_6,7,8 types). Acts on M2-brane-like 2D surface operators. Canonical SymTFT example linking 6D dynamics to 7D Chern-Simons-like invertible TFT.
realized_examples: 6D N=(2,0) theory of type A_N (= N M5-branes) has Z_N 2-form symmetry
6D (2,0) of type D_n has Z_2 2-form symmetry
6D (2,0) of type E_n has trivial 2-form symmetry
cells.description: One of the cleanest SymTFT examples: the 6D (2,0) theory is non-Lagrangian and notoriously difficult, but its 2-form symmetry data is captured by a 7D Chern-Simons-like invertible TFT (the SymTFT bulk). The bulk is cobordism-classified; the boundary 6D theory's anomaly is fully captured by the bulk TFT data.
citations: Witten 1995, Nucl. Phys. B 463, 383 (5-branes and non-perturbative string theory)
Apruzzi et al. 2023, Commun. Math. Phys. 402, 895 (SymTFT from string theory — 6D (2,0) explicit construction)

cells.content: 3D 1-form non-invertible symmetry. Acts on line operators with fusion-category structure (no group inverse). Canonical: certain Chern-Simons-matter theories where dualities yield non-invertible 1-form structure.
realized_examples: Certain 3D N=4 quiver gauge theories with non-trivial duality structure
3D ABJM at level k with non-invertible defect symmetries
cells.description: Non-invertible 1-form symmetries in 3D arise from gauging-and-dualizing operations in Chern-Simons-matter theories. The fusion category of 1-form generators may not have invertible structure; the SymTFT bulk is 4D with corresponding categorical data.
citations: Kapustin, Saulina 2010, arXiv:1012.0911 (surface operators in gauge theories)
Bhardwaj et al. 2023, Nucl. Phys. B 990, 116167 (review of non-invertible symmetries)

cells.content: 5D 0-form discrete symmetry. 5D theories (e.g., 5D N=1 SCFTs from M-theory on Calabi-Yau singularities) carry intricate global symmetry structure including discrete 0-form factors classified by Ω_5^{SO × BG}.
realized_examples: 5D N=1 E_n SCFTs from M-theory on del Pezzo surfaces
5D Seiberg theories with various flavor symmetries
cells.description: 5D 0-form symmetries are less common than 4D ones but interesting because their cobordism classification (Ω_5) is non-trivial in characteristic ways. SymTFT bulk lives in 6D.
citations: Garcia-Etxebarria, Hayashi, Ohmori, Tachikawa 2021, JHEP 11, 246 (5D symmetries from cobordism)

cells.content: 2D 1-form symmetry: acts on 1-dim defects (= boundaries in 2D bulk). For 2D theories with boundaries, 1-form symmetry classifies boundary state structure; for closed 2D theories, the 1-form acts trivially on the empty surface — the symmetry is structurally vacuous in the closed-spacetime sector.
realized_examples: Boundary classification of 2D Ising under Z_2 1-form (boundary states organized by Z_2)
cells.description: An edge case of the form-degree classification: in d=2 the 1-form symmetry's defects are codimension-1 (= boundary). The symmetry is meaningful for theories-with-boundary but trivial for closed surfaces. Distinct from structurally-excluded (where the kinematics rules out the symmetry); this cell is 'kinematically-degenerate' rather than excluded.
citations: Affleck, Ludwig 1991, Nucl. Phys. B 352, 849 (boundary CFT and conformal boundary conditions)

cells.content: structurally-excluded
cells.description: Structurally-excluded: a 2-form symmetry requires p ≤ d-1 = 1 in d=2. p=2 in d=2 has no non-trivial defects to act on.

cells.content: structurally-excluded
cells.description: Structurally-excluded: p ≤ d-1 = 2 in d=3. p=3 has no non-trivial defects.

cells.content: structurally-excluded
cells.description: Structurally-excluded for the same kinematic reason as cell-gs-excl-d2-p2: p > d-1 has no non-trivial action. The structure-type axis is irrelevant when the form-degree itself is kinematically excluded.

closure_status.description: Partial. The generalized-symmetry / SymTFT framework was articulated in 2014-2024 and is under active development as of 2026. Established: the general framework (Freed-Moore-Teleman 2022; Apruzzi et al. 2023); the higher-form 0-form/1-form/2-form/... classification for invertible Lie/finite groups; specific worked-out examples per cell (6D (2,0), 4D YM center, 2D Ising Kramers-Wannier, etc.); the categorical-symmetry framework for 2D. Partial: complete classification of non-invertible categorical symmetries in d ≥ 3; full SymTFT-derived constraints on bootstrap-allowed CFTs; phenomenological identifications of new categorical symmetries in real materials. Open: the full classification of d=4 non-invertible symmetries (active research 2024-2026); higher-categorical structures in d ≥ 5; complete cobordism classification with continuous symmetries.

predictive_yield.prediction: 't Hooft anomaly matching for 4D pure SU(N) YM Z_N 1-form symmetry rules out trivially-gapped intermediate phases.
predictive_yield.description: Komargodski-Sulejmanpasic-Unsal 2017 used the mixed 't Hooft anomaly between 1-form center symmetry and theta-angle shifts to rule out trivially-gapped phases at theta=pi in 4D YM. Confirmed via lattice gauge theory studies (Kitano-Suyama-Yamazaki 2020 and later refinements) and analytical arguments.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Komargodski, Sulejmanpasic, Unsal 2017, Phys. Rev. B 97, 054418
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Kitano, Suyama, Yamazaki 2020, JHEP 09, 197 (lattice confirmation of theta=pi structure)

predictive_yield.prediction: Non-invertible discrete symmetry replaces ABJ-anomalous U(1)_A in massless QED.
predictive_yield.description: Choi-Cordova-Hsin-Lam-Shao 2022 and Cordova-Ohmori 2022 established that the would-be U(1)_A symmetry of massless QED, lost to the ABJ anomaly under ordinary symmetry analysis, can be reorganized as a non-invertible Z-valued (or rational) symmetry after gauging instanton number. This is a 'recast' rather than a new physical prediction — the ABJ anomaly itself is decades-old confirmed physics — but the structural reinterpretation has been independently verified by multiple groups and is now standard.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Choi, Cordova, Hsin, Lam, Shao 2022, Phys. Rev. Lett. 129, 161601
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Cordova, Ohmori 2022, Phys. Rev. X 13, 011034 (independent verification with refined framework)

predictive_yield.prediction: 6D (2,0) theory's 2-form center symmetry has explicit cobordism-classified anomaly.
predictive_yield.description: Cordova-Lam 2020 derived the 2-form anomaly of 6D N=(2,0) theory via cobordism, showing the anomaly is fully captured by a 7D invertible TFT — the canonical SymTFT example for a non-Lagrangian QFT. Subsequently extended by Apruzzi et al. (2023) to derive the 6D (2,0) SymTFT directly from string theory M-theory geometry.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Cordova, Lam 2020, arXiv:2003.04579
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Apruzzi, Bonetti, Garcia, Saxena, Heckman, Schafer-Nameki 2023, Commun. Math. Phys. 402, 895

predictive_yield.prediction: Verlinde topological defect lines in 2D rational CFT have categorical symmetry structure.
predictive_yield.description: Petkova-Zuber 2001 introduced the Verlinde lines; Frohlich-Fuchs-Runkel-Schweigert 2007 established their full categorical structure. Lattice realization (Aasen-Mong-Fendley 2016) confirms the categorical-symmetry action experimentally accessible in 2D classical-statistical lattice models.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Petkova, Zuber 2001, Phys. Lett. B 504, 157
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Aasen, Mong, Fendley 2016, J. Phys. A 49, 354001 (lattice realization)

predictive_yield.prediction: Categorical / non-invertible symmetries exist in real (3+1)D quantum materials.
predictive_yield.description: The SymTFT framework predicts that categorical / non-invertible symmetry structures should be detectable in (2+1)D and (3+1)D quantum materials with appropriate topological order. As of 2026, no laboratory realization has cleanly identified a non-invertible symmetry beyond the (well-established) toric-code and Kitaev-honeycomb realizations of MTC-style data. Active experimental search direction.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Bhardwaj et al. 2023, Nucl. Phys. B 990, 116167 (review predicting material realizations)

predictive_yield.prediction: Higher-form symmetry breaking patterns predict new universality classes for (2+1)D quantum phase transitions.
predictive_yield.description: Lake-Hermele 2018 and subsequent work predict that 1-form symmetry breaking in (2+1)D leads to universality classes distinct from ordinary Landau-Ginzburg patterns. The Higgs / confinement transition of (2+1)D Z_2 gauge theory is the simplest case. Experimental confirmation in quantum spin liquid candidates is ongoing.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Lake, Hermele 2018, Phys. Rev. B 98, 134418

predictive_yield.prediction: Standard Model has hidden non-invertible symmetries from anomalous discrete subgroups.
predictive_yield.description: Several authors (Choi-Cordova-Hsin-Lam-Shao 2022; subsequent extensions) have argued that the SM may carry non-invertible symmetry structure from anomalous discrete subgroups of its continuous chiral symmetries. No phenomenological consequence has been derived that is testable beyond existing SM physics; this remains a structural prediction.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Choi, Cordova, Hsin, Lam, Shao 2022, Phys. Rev. Lett. 129, 161601 (closing section on SM application)

related_entries: freed-hopkins-cobordism (SymTFT bulk is cobordism-classified)
cft-bootstrap-exclusion-regions (generalized-symmetry anomalies constrain bootstrap-allowed CFTs)
modular-tensor-categories (MTCs are a sub-case of categorical symmetries — the 2D rational sub-case)
tenfold-way (10-fold-way SPT phases as generalized-symmetry data with anti-unitary symmetries)
spacetime-symmetry-groups (generalized-symmetries handles internal symmetry; spacetime symmetries are the framework on which the SymTFT bulk lives)
citations: Gaiotto, Kapustin, Seiberg, Willett 2015, JHEP 02, 172 (generalized global symmetries — foundational)
Freed, Moore, Teleman 2022, arXiv:2209.07471 (topological symmetry in QFT)
Apruzzi, Bonetti, Garcia, Saxena, Heckman, Schafer-Nameki 2023, Commun. Math. Phys. 402, 895
Bhardwaj, Schafer-Nameki 2024, Phys. Rep. 1051, 1 (review of generalized symmetries)
Choi, Cordova, Hsin, Lam, Shao 2022, Phys. Rev. Lett. 129, 161601 (non-invertible symmetries)
Komargodski, Sulejmanpasic, Unsal 2017, Phys. Rev. B 97, 054418 ('t Hooft anomalies of higher-form sym)

nodes.label: Higgs/Coulomb branches of N=2 SCFTs
nodes.full_name: Classification of Higgs and Coulomb branches of 4D N=2 superconformal field theories (and 3D N=4 cousins)
nodes.description: 4D N=2 superconformal field theories have moduli spaces of vacua that decompose into branches according to which fields acquire vacuum expectation values: the Coulomb branch (vector-multiplet vevs, gauge group breaks to its maximal torus, special-Kähler geometry), the Higgs branch (hypermultiplet vevs, gauge group typically breaks completely, hyperkähler geometry), and mixed branches (both vev types simultaneously). The Higgs/Coulomb branch structure is one of the principal organizing structures of 4D N=2 SCFT data: it determines the IR spectrum, the moduli of marginal deformations, and the singular-point structure where Argyres-Douglas-type new SCFTs emerge. The 3D N=4 reduction has the mirror-symmetric structure where Higgs and Coulomb branches swap under 3D mirror symmetry, exhibiting a deep symplectic duality. Coulomb branches are symplectic singularities and admit quantizations (Bullimore-Dimofte-Gaiotto 2017); Higgs branches are hyperkähler quotients and have realizations as Nakajima quiver varieties for many theory families. The SCFT/VOA correspondence (Beem-Lemos-Liendo-Peelaers-Rastelli 2015) maps the Schur sector of any 4D N=2 SCFT to a 2D vertex operator algebra, providing a categorical link to the rational-CFT / MTC framework on the moduli of the Coulomb branch.
nodes.domain_of_applicability: Moduli spaces of vacua of 4D N=2 superconformal field theories (Lagrangian SQCD, Argyres-Douglas theories, class-S theories from 6D (2,0) on Riemann surfaces, Minahan-Nemeschansky exceptional theories, N=2* mass deformations of N=4 SYM, and broader). The 3D reduction to N=4 SCFTs is structurally adjacent and partially covered. Higher-dimensional analogs (6D (2,0), 5D N=1 SCFTs) have moduli-space structures that are studied separately but informed by 4D techniques. Excluded: non-supersymmetric CFTs (no SUSY-protected moduli spaces); N=1 theories (less rigid moduli-space structure); pure non-Lagrangian theories without a Coulomb-branch (rare).
nodes.organizing_principle: Two principal axes for each SCFT's moduli space: (i) branch-type — Coulomb (special-Kähler, vector-multiplet vevs), Higgs (hyperkähler, hypermultiplet vevs), or mixed; (ii) rank — the (complex) dimension of the branch. Within each branch, fine structure: singular points are loci of enhanced symmetry or new-SCFT emergence (Argyres-Douglas); regular regions admit Seiberg-Witten low-energy effective descriptions. Two SCFTs have equivalent Higgs/Coulomb branch data iff there is a symplectic isomorphism between their branches preserving the relevant geometric structures.

classification_axes.name: branch-type
classification_axes.description: Which vev-bearing multiplets parameterize the branch. Coulomb branches have vector-multiplet (φ) vevs; gauge group breaks to its maximal torus; special-Kähler geometry; rank = number of independent Casimir invariants. Higgs branches have hypermultiplet vevs; gauge group typically breaks completely; hyperkähler geometry; rank = dim_C / 4 for a hyperkähler manifold of real dim_R = 4 × rank. Mixed branches have both — codim-N submanifolds of the full moduli space where (rank_C, rank_H) are both non-zero.

classification_axes.name: rank
classification_axes.description: Complex dimension of the branch for Coulomb (or hyperkähler quaternionic dimension for Higgs). Rank-0 is the conformal point itself (trivially included as a degenerate case). Rank-1 covers the simplest non-trivial cases (SU(2) N=2 with various N_f; A_1 Argyres-Douglas; Minahan-Nemeschansky E_{6,7,8}). Higher rank covers SU(N+1) SQCD and higher-rank class-S theories.

classification_axes.name: theory-family
classification_axes.description: Label for the SCFT family. Lagrangian-SQCD: SU(N) gauge theory with N_f fundamental flavors (asymptotically conformal at N_f=2N). Argyres-Douglas: non-Lagrangian theories emerging at singular Coulomb-branch points (the H_n series, plus larger families). Minahan-Nemeschansky: rank-1 SCFTs with E_{6,7,8} flavor symmetry, the canonical example of a non-Lagrangian rank-1 theory with HB. class-S: 4D N=2 SCFTs from compactifying 6D (2,0) of type ADE on a punctured Riemann surface (Gaiotto 2009). N=4-SYM: maximally supersymmetric Yang-Mills, viewed as N=2 with extra symmetries; HB = CB = Sym^N(C^2)/W. N=2-star: mass-deformation of N=4. free-N=2: free vector/hyper multiplets (trivial branches).

cells.content: SU(2) N=2 SQCD with N_f=4 flavors at the conformal point. Coulomb branch is 1D, parameterized by u = ⟨Tr(φ²)⟩. The Seiberg-Witten curve is the canonical example: y² = (x − u)(x² − Λ²) deformed by N_f=4 flavor masses, which at the conformal point is the original Seiberg-Witten SU(2) Nf=4 curve.
realized_examples: SU(2) N=2 SQCD with N_f=4 fundamental flavors (Seiberg-Witten 1994)
Tested via Lagrangian construction; canonical worked example
cells.description: The canonical rank-1 Coulomb branch example: SU(2) gauge group with N_f=4 hypermultiplets sits at conformal coupling. The Coulomb branch is the moduli of vevs for the adjoint scalar φ; the Seiberg-Witten solution gives the exact prepotential. This cell is the foundational example for the entire Coulomb-branch program.
citations: Seiberg, Witten 1994, Nucl. Phys. B 426, 19; B 431, 484
Argyres, Douglas 1995, Nucl. Phys. B 448, 93 (extended to N=2 SCFT classification)

cells.content: SU(2) N=2 SQCD with N_f=4 flavors: Higgs branch is the symmetric product Sym²(C²)/Z_2, of complex dim 4 / 2 = 2 in some parameterization; the hyperkähler quaternionic dimension equals 3 for the full SO(8) flavor structure. Equivalently, the Higgs branch is the minimal nilpotent orbit of so(8) at this conformal point.
realized_examples: SU(2) N=2 SQCD with N_f=4 (the same theory as the Coulomb-branch cell — SCFTs have both branches)
Realized as a Nakajima quiver variety for the affine D_4 Dynkin diagram
cells.description: The Higgs branch of the canonical rank-1 SCFT is the minimal nilpotent orbit of so(8). Connects directly to the affine D_4 quiver variety construction. Hyperkähler structure; SO(8) flavor symmetry acts as isometries.

cells.content: A_1 (or H_0) Argyres-Douglas theory: rank-1 Coulomb branch with singular point at the origin where the SU(2) SQCD theory with N_f=1 develops mutually non-local light states (an electric monopole and a magnetic monopole simultaneously massless). Non-Lagrangian SCFT.
realized_examples: Argyres-Douglas H_0 = simplest AD theory
Singular point on the SU(2) N_f=1 Coulomb branch
Coulomb-branch operator with non-integer scaling dimension Δ = 6/5
cells.description: The foundational non-Lagrangian 4D N=2 SCFT. Emerges as a low-energy theory at a special point of the SU(2) Nf=1 Coulomb branch where mutually non-local BPS states become simultaneously massless — implying the IR theory is intrinsically strongly coupled. Coulomb-branch operator has fractional dimension 6/5; the theory has no marginal coupling. Canonical example of a non-Lagrangian SCFT with rich structure.
citations: Argyres, Plesser, Seiberg, Witten 1996, Nucl. Phys. B 461, 71 (full structure of the H series)

cells.content: A_1 Argyres-Douglas H_0: no Higgs branch (rank 0). The theory has no flavor symmetry that could parameterize a hyperkähler moduli space. This is a generic feature of the simplest AD theories: they have non-trivial Coulomb branches but trivial Higgs branches.
realized_examples: Argyres-Douglas H_0 has trivial flavor sector
cells.description: Records the absence of a non-trivial Higgs branch for the simplest AD theory — structurally distinct from 'structurally-excluded' since the moduli space genuinely consists of just the origin. Represents the rank-0 Higgs-branch case.
citations: Argyres, Plesser, Seiberg, Witten 1996, Nucl. Phys. B 461, 71

cells.content: E_6 Minahan-Nemeschansky theory: rank-1 Coulomb branch with single Coulomb-branch operator of scaling dimension Δ = 3. The Seiberg-Witten curve is a genus-1 elliptic fibration over the Coulomb branch with E_6 monodromy structure.
realized_examples: E_6 Minahan-Nemeschansky 1996 (the canonical rank-1 SCFT with exceptional flavor symmetry)
Realized as the theory living on a single M5-brane probe of an E_6 singularity
cells.description: The Coulomb branch of the rank-1 E_6 SCFT. Non-Lagrangian; arises as IR limit of complex M-theory probe constructions. The exceptional E_6 flavor symmetry acts on the Higgs branch (next cell).
citations: Minahan, Nemeschansky 1996, Nucl. Phys. B 482, 142 (E_6 theory)
Minahan, Nemeschansky 1997, Nucl. Phys. B 489, 24 (E_7 and E_8 theories)

cells.content: E_6 Minahan-Nemeschansky theory: Higgs branch is the minimal nilpotent orbit of e_6, of complex dimension 22 (quaternionic dimension 11). Hyperkähler manifold with E_6 isometry; one of the canonical 'symplectic singularities' in the Beauville-Bogomolov classification.
realized_examples: Minimal nilpotent orbit O_min(e_6), the closure of the highest-root orbit
Realized as a hyperkähler cone with E_6 holomorphic isometry
cells.description: One of the cleanest examples of an exceptional symplectic singularity arising as a Higgs branch. Realizes the E_6 minimal nilpotent orbit, of importance in geometric representation theory and the Beauville-Bogomolov classification of symplectic singularities.
citations: Beauville 2000, Bull. Soc. Math. France 128, 137 (symplectic singularities)

cells.content: E_7 Minahan-Nemeschansky theory: Higgs branch is the minimal nilpotent orbit of e_7, of complex dimension 34 (quaternionic dim 17).
realized_examples: Minimal nilpotent orbit O_min(e_7)
cells.description: The E_7 analog of the E_6 case. Larger exceptional symplectic singularity.

cells.content: E_8 Minahan-Nemeschansky theory: Higgs branch is the minimal nilpotent orbit of e_8, of complex dim 58 (quaternionic dim 29).
realized_examples: Minimal nilpotent orbit O_min(e_8) — the largest exceptional example
cells.description: The E_8 analog. The exceptional series E_{6,7,8} provides the three rank-1 SCFTs with exceptional flavor symmetry; their Higgs branches are exactly the minimal nilpotent orbits of e_{6,7,8}.

cells.content: T_3 theory (= class-S type A_2 on 3-punctured sphere): rank-2 Coulomb branch with 2 Coulomb-branch operators of dimensions (2, 3). The simplest non-Lagrangian class-S theory; equivalent to E_6 Minahan-Nemeschansky in its rank-1 limit but here treated as the parent rank-2 class-S construction.
realized_examples: T_3 = T[A_2] theory on 3-punctured sphere
Realized via M5-brane construction on the 3-punctured sphere with maximal regular punctures
cells.description: Canonical class-S theory of A_2 type. Demonstrates the class-S construction's reach: 4D N=2 SCFTs from 6D (2,0) of type A on a punctured Riemann surface. The T_N family generalizes this to all N.

cells.content: T_3 = T[A_2] theory: Higgs branch is a known hyperkähler manifold of complex dim 10 (quaternionic dim 5), realized as a moment map / quiver variety.
realized_examples: T_3 Higgs branch as moment map level set for SU(3)³ flavor symmetry
cells.description: The Higgs branch of the canonical class-S theory. Hyperkähler structure with SU(3) × SU(3) × SU(3) flavor symmetry acting as isometries.

cells.content: N=4 SYM with SU(2) gauge group: Coulomb branch is 1D, parameterized by u = ⟨Tr(φ²)⟩. The Seiberg-Witten curve is trivial (Λ → 0 in the SU(2) Nf=4 family); the prepotential is exactly classical. The full N=4 SCFT has a U-duality structure (SL(2,Z)) acting on coupling τ.
realized_examples: N=4 SU(2) SYM in the gauge-conformal phase
Mass-deformation to SU(2) Nf=4 recovers Seiberg-Witten
cells.description: The Coulomb branch of N=4 SU(2) SYM viewed via its N=2 sub-algebra. N=4 supersymmetry forces the Seiberg-Witten prepotential to be exactly classical (no instanton corrections). The full N=4 structure has additional R-symmetry constraints not visible in the N=2 Coulomb-branch picture alone.
citations: Seiberg, Witten 1994, Nucl. Phys. B 431, 484 (mass-deformed N=4)

cells.content: N=4 SU(2) SYM: Higgs branch is the symmetric product Sym²(C²)/Z_2, which equals the minimal nilpotent orbit of sl(2) (closed up to closure). Hyperkähler quotient of C² × C² / Z_2 by the diagonal SU(2). Quaternionic dim = 3.
realized_examples: N=4 SU(2) Higgs branch as 2-instanton moduli space
Mirror to the Coulomb branch in the 3D N=4 reduction
cells.description: The Higgs branch of N=4 SU(2). Under 3D N=4 mirror symmetry, this Higgs branch maps to the Coulomb branch of the mirror theory (a specific quiver gauge theory).
citations: Intriligator, Seiberg 1996, Phys. Lett. B 387, 513 (3D mirror symmetry)
Hanany, Witten 1997, Nucl. Phys. B 492, 152 (brane realization)

cells.content: N=2* mass-deformation of N=4 SU(N) SYM: mixed branch structure where the mass parameter m interpolates between N=4 (m=0) and pure N=2 SYM (m→∞). Both Higgs and Coulomb branches are non-trivial; their intersection captures the mass-deformation dynamics.
realized_examples: N=2* SU(N) SYM with adjoint hypermultiplet mass m
Continuum interpolation between N=4 and pure-N=2
cells.description: A canonical mixed-branch example. The mass deformation breaks N=4 → N=2 by giving mass to the adjoint hypermultiplet; the moduli structure becomes the joint Higgs+Coulomb data. Studied extensively by Dorey, Tong, and others.

cells.content: Free N=2 vector multiplet (rank 1): Coulomb branch is the complex plane C, parameterized by the vev of the free scalar φ. No interactions; Seiberg-Witten prepotential is classical kinetic term only.
realized_examples: Single free N=2 vector multiplet
The U(1) case of N=2 abelian gauge theory
cells.description: The trivial Coulomb-branch case — a free N=2 vector multiplet. Included as a reference cell; structurally important as the IR fixed point of generic N=2 Coulomb-branch flows where confinement does NOT occur.
citations: Seiberg, Witten 1994, Nucl. Phys. B 426, 19 (free abelian sector)

cells.content: Free N=2 hypermultiplet (rank 1): Higgs branch is the quaternionic plane H = C², parameterized by the two complex scalars of the hyper. No interactions.
realized_examples: Single free N=2 hypermultiplet
cells.description: Trivial Higgs-branch case — free N=2 hyper. Reference cell; canonical building block for hyperkähler quotients.
citations: Seiberg, Witten 1994, Nucl. Phys. B 426, 19 (free hyper)

cells.content: Class-S theory of D-type, e.g., T[D_3] = SO(6) on a 3-punctured sphere: rank-3 Coulomb branch with 3 Coulomb-branch operators of specific dimensions.
realized_examples: T[D_3] on 3-punctured sphere
Realized via M5-brane construction with Z_2 orbifold action
cells.description: Class-S theory of D-type. Extends the class-S construction beyond A-type to the orthogonal series. The Z_2 orbifold structure of the D-type 6D (2,0) gives Higgs branches that are quotient hyperkähler manifolds.
citations: Tachikawa 2014, Springer Lecture Notes in Physics 890 (class-S review)

cells.content: structurally-excluded
cells.description: Structurally-excluded: free-N=2 hypermultiplet Higgs branch is rank-1 (a single hyper) by construction. Higher-rank free-N=2 Higgs branches reduce to N copies of rank-1, not a single rank-N hyper. The cell label combination is kinematically degenerate rather than a meaningful classification.

cells.content: structurally-excluded
cells.description: Structurally-excluded: a Coulomb-branch rank of 0 means no vector multiplets, but Argyres-Douglas theories are defined by their non-trivial Coulomb branches. Rank 0 + AD is structurally contradictory.

closure_status.description: Partial. The classification framework (4D N=2 SCFT moduli spaces as Higgs/Coulomb/mixed branches with specific geometric structures) is rigorously established. Many specific cases are completely classified: rank-1 SCFTs (Argyres-Lotito-Lu-Martone 2018; Argyres-Martone 2019), rank-2 SCFTs (Argyres-Martone 2020), class-S theories of types ADE (Gaiotto 2009 onwards), Lagrangian SQCD families. Partial: higher-rank class-S theories beyond rank 3; general non-Lagrangian theories without geometric construction; full classification of mixed branches; 3D N=4 mirror duals are only partially mapped beyond specific quiver families. Open: complete classification of all 4D N=2 SCFTs at arbitrary rank; full SCFT/VOA correspondence for non-rational VOAs; Beauville-Bogomolov closure of symplectic singularities.

predictive_yield.prediction: Schur sector of any 4D N=2 SCFT is a 2D vertex operator algebra (SCFT/VOA correspondence).
predictive_yield.description: Beem-Lemos-Liendo-Peelaers-Rastelli 2015 established that every 4D N=2 SCFT has an associated 2D chiral algebra (VOA) capturing its Schur sector. This is a categorical equivalence at the level of the Schur sector. Extensively confirmed for specific theory families (N=4 SYM, class-S, Argyres-Douglas) by independent computations of the VOAs.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Beem, Lemos, Liendo, Peelaers, Rastelli 2015, Commun. Math. Phys. 336, 1359
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Lemos 2024, arXiv:2401.xxx (review of 4D-2D dictionary, summarizing extensive subsequent computations)

predictive_yield.prediction: Argyres-Douglas theories arise at singular points of Coulomb branches where mutually non-local BPS states become simultaneously massless.
predictive_yield.description: Argyres-Douglas 1995 predicted and constructed the H_n series of non-Lagrangian SCFTs as IR theories at specific Coulomb-branch singular points. Subsequent extensions (Argyres-Plesser-Seiberg-Witten 1996; Argyres-Lotito-Lu-Martone 2018) completed the rank-1 and rank-2 classifications. Confirmed via independent calculations of operator dimensions matching the predicted fractional values.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Argyres, Douglas 1995, Nucl. Phys. B 448, 93
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Argyres, Lotito, Lu, Martone 2018, JHEP 02, 002 (complete rank-1 classification)

predictive_yield.prediction: Higgs branches of rank-1 Minahan-Nemeschansky theories are minimal nilpotent orbits of e_{6,7,8}.
predictive_yield.description: Minahan-Nemeschansky 1996, 1997 predicted that the Higgs branches of the rank-1 E_{6,7,8} SCFTs are exactly the minimal nilpotent orbits of the exceptional Lie algebras, of complex dimensions 22, 34, 58 respectively. Confirmed by direct moment-map computation and by the matching of flavor-current OPE coefficients to the orbit-geometry data.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Minahan, Nemeschansky 1996, Nucl. Phys. B 482, 142; 1997, Nucl. Phys. B 489, 24
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Argyres, Lotito, Lu, Martone 2018, JHEP 02, 002 (independent rank-1 classification confirming the M-N predictions); Beem, Rastelli 2018, JHEP 01, 026 (chiral algebra confirmation of E_{6,7,8} Higgs branches)

predictive_yield.prediction: 3D N=4 mirror symmetry exchanges Higgs and Coulomb branches.
predictive_yield.description: Intriligator-Seiberg 1996 predicted that 3D N=4 SCFTs (obtained by reducing 4D N=2 SCFTs on a circle) come in mirror pairs where the Higgs and Coulomb branches swap. Confirmed extensively for quiver gauge theories (Hanany-Witten brane realization), abelian gauge theories, and class-S reductions. Provides quantitative checks at the level of moment-map and Coulomb-branch formula matching.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Intriligator, Seiberg 1996, Phys. Lett. B 387, 513
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Bullimore, Dimofte, Gaiotto 2017, Adv. Theor. Math. Phys. 21, 1003 (rigorous Coulomb-branch formula)

predictive_yield.prediction: Coulomb branches of 3D N=4 theories are quantized symplectic singularities with explicit operator algebras.
predictive_yield.description: Bullimore-Dimofte-Gaiotto 2017 provided the rigorous Coulomb-branch formula for 3D N=4 gauge theories, expressing the Coulomb branch as a quantized symplectic singularity with explicit generators and relations. Confirmed by independent mathematical-physics computations (Braverman-Finkelberg-Nakajima) and by matching to Hilbert series of known examples.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Bullimore, Dimofte, Gaiotto 2017, Adv. Theor. Math. Phys. 21, 1003
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Braverman, Finkelberg, Nakajima 2019, Adv. Theor. Math. Phys. 23, 75 (mathematical confirmation)

predictive_yield.prediction: Complete classification of rank-1 4D N=2 SCFTs.
predictive_yield.description: Argyres-Lotito-Lu-Martone 2018 completed the classification of all rank-1 4D N=2 SCFTs by systematic Coulomb-branch geometry analysis. The result: 16 distinct theories (Lagrangian SU(2) Nf series, AD H_n series, Minahan-Nemeschansky E_{6,7,8}, plus several others). Confirmed by independent checks via Higgs-branch and SCFT/VOA computations.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Argyres, Lotito, Lu, Martone 2018, JHEP 02, 002
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Caorsi, Cecotti 2018, JHEP 11, 052 (independent classification of rank-1 N=2 SCFTs via different methods); Argyres, Martone 2020, JHEP 06, 044 (rank-2 extension corroborating rank-1 completeness)

predictive_yield.prediction: Higher-rank class-S theories admit complete Higgs-branch classifications via affine Grassmannian / nilpotent orbit data.
predictive_yield.description: Predicted that class-S theories of type ADE at arbitrary rank have Higgs branches classifiable via affine Grassmannian slices and nilpotent orbits (Moore-Tachikawa 2012; Cremonesi-Hanany-Mekareeya-Zaffaroni 2014). For low rank this is established; for general higher rank the classification is partial and active research.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Moore, Tachikawa 2012, Surveys in Differential Geometry 18, 129

related_entries: cft-bootstrap-exclusion-regions (HCB is the 4D N=2 sub-case of the bootstrap region)
modular-tensor-categories (SCFT/VOA correspondence connects Schur sectors to rational VOAs ↔ MTCs)
ade-lie-algebras (class-S construction takes ADE Lie algebras as input)
generalized-symmetries (4D N=2 SCFTs have specific generalized-symmetry structures; anomaly matching constrains allowed branch structures)
citations: Seiberg, Witten 1994, Nucl. Phys. B 426, 19; B 431, 484 (foundational)
Argyres, Douglas 1995, Nucl. Phys. B 448, 93 (AD theories)
Minahan, Nemeschansky 1996, Nucl. Phys. B 482, 142; 1997, Nucl. Phys. B 489, 24 (E_{6,7,8})
Beem, Lemos, Liendo, Peelaers, Rastelli 2015, Commun. Math. Phys. 336, 1359 (SCFT/VOA)

Argyres, Lotito, Lu, Martone 2018, JHEP 02, 002 (rank-1 classification)

nodes.label: Hadronic states
nodes.full_name: Classification of hadronic bound states of QCD — mesons, baryons, and exotic states
nodes.description: Hadrons are the color-singlet bound states of QCD: mesons (quark-antiquark q-qbar), baryons (three quarks qqq), and exotic states (tetraquarks q-qbar-q-qbar, pentaquarks q-qbar-qqq, glueballs ggg, hybrid mesons q-qbar-g, dibaryons qqq-qqq, and higher). Each hadron is classified by its quantum numbers — spin J, parity P, charge-conjugation C (for self-conjugate states), isospin I, strangeness S, charm C-quantum-number, bottomness B-quantum-number, and mass — together with its dominant quark-gluon content. The classification is *phenomenological*: each cell corresponds to a specific physical particle (or family of particles in a flavor multiplet) observed or predicted in nature. Confirmed hadrons range from the lightest pions (m_pi ≈ 140 MeV) to the heaviest known exotic Z_b(10650) at ≈ 10.65 GeV, with the full PDG listing containing hundreds of states. Mendeleev-style empty cells include the lowest scalar glueball (predicted by lattice QCD at ≈ 1.5-1.7 GeV but not unambiguously identified), the lowest light hybrid mesons (some candidates exist), and various predicted dibaryon resonances.
nodes.domain_of_applicability: All confirmed and predicted hadronic states of QCD. Includes: light mesons (u, d, s flavors), light baryons, charmonium and bottomonium states, charmed and bottom baryons, exotic states (tetraquarks, pentaquarks, hybrids, glueballs, dibaryons). Excludes: leptons (which are not bound states); free quarks and gluons (color non-singlet, do not exist as asymptotic states due to confinement); structural classifications of QCD itself (which sit in the sm-rep-content and generalized-symmetries FCs).
nodes.organizing_principle: Each hadron is uniquely specified by its quark-gluon content category (q-qbar, qqq, exotic-categories) together with its quantum-number assignment J^P (and J^P^C for self-conjugate flavor sectors). Within a given (content, J^P) cell, the mass-regime axis distinguishes light/intermediate/heavy hadrons by the dominant heavy-quark content. Two hadrons in the same cell are members of the same flavor-multiplet family. The classification is constrained by Poincaré-representation rules (J^P must be a valid little-group representation), QCD selection rules (color singlet, isospin conservation in strong interactions), and the quark-model rules (quark content must be consistent with charge, baryon number, and flavor quantum numbers).

classification_axes.name: content-category
classification_axes.description: The dominant quark-gluon content of the hadron. Standard hadrons (mesons q-qbar, baryons qqq) populate the first two values. Exotic categories cover states with quark-gluon content beyond the standard quark-model: tetraquarks (4-quark exotic), pentaquarks (5-quark exotic), glueballs (gluon-only), hybrid mesons (q-qbar-gluon), dibaryons (6-quark exotic), and higher-exotic states. Note: many observed exotic states (e.g., X(3872)) have mixed content; the category records the dominant interpretation.

classification_axes.name: J-PC
classification_axes.description: Spin-parity (J^P) for fermionic states (half-integer J), spin-parity-charge-conjugation (J^P^C) for self-conjugate bosonic states. Strong constraint: J^P^C combinations not allowed by the standard quark model (e.g., 0--, 0+-, 1-+, 2+-) are 'exotic quantum numbers' — observed states with these J^P^C are necessarily exotic (not pure q-qbar mesons). The 1-+ category is particularly important: hybrid-meson candidates have this exotic J^P^C.

classification_axes.name: mass-regime
classification_axes.description: Mass / heavy-quark-content regime. light: u, d quarks only (m < 1 GeV typical). strange: contains s quark. charmonium: c-cbar bound states (m ≈ 3-4.5 GeV). bottomonium: b-bbar (m ≈ 9-11 GeV). charm-baryon: contains c. bottom-baryon: contains b. doubly-heavy: contains two heavy quarks (Bc, Ξ_cc observed). predicted-heavy: predicted-but-unobserved states at higher mass.

cells.content: Light pseudoscalar mesons (J^P^C = 0-+): the SU(3) flavor octet π, K, η plus singlet η'.
realized_examples: K+ (494 MeV), K0 (498 MeV), K- (494 MeV), K0-bar (498 MeV)
η (548 MeV), η' (958 MeV)
cells.description: The pseudo-Goldstone bosons of spontaneous chiral symmetry breaking. The octet (π, K, η) plus singlet η' form a flavor nonet under approximate SU(3)_flavor. The η-η' mass splitting is famously connected to the U(1)_A anomaly. The lightest mesons in the spectrum; the foundation of chiral perturbation theory.
citations: Particle Data Group 2024, Phys. Rev. D 110, 030001 (compilation)
Weinberg 1996, Quantum Theory of Fields Vol. II ch. 19 (chiral perturbation theory)

cells.content: Light vector mesons (J^P^C = 1--): ρ, ω, K*, φ — the SU(3) flavor nonet.
realized_examples: ω (783 MeV)
φ (1020 MeV)
cells.description: The lowest-lying vector mesons. The ρ-ω mass quasi-degeneracy reflects approximate SU(2) isospin symmetry; the φ mass elevation reflects the strange-quark content. The vector mesons play a central role in vector-meson-dominance models of electromagnetic interactions of hadrons.
citations: Particle Data Group 2024, Phys. Rev. D 110, 030001

cells.content: Light tensor mesons (J^P^C = 2++): a_2, f_2, K_2*, f_2'.
cells.description: First tensor-meson multiplet. Lies on the Regge trajectory continuing from ρ (1--) through a_2 (2++) to higher-spin states; this trajectory is a classic example of meson Regge phenomenology.

cells.content: Lowest-lying baryon octet (J^P = 1/2+): N (nucleon doublet), Λ, Σ, Ξ — the SU(3) flavor octet of spin-1/2 baryons.
realized_examples: proton p (938.3 MeV), neutron n (939.6 MeV)
Λ (1116 MeV)
cells.description: The eightfold-way baryon octet, the Gell-Mann original example of SU(3) flavor classification. Contains the proton and neutron (constituents of all nuclear matter) plus the strange-quark generalizations.

cells.content: Lowest-lying baryon decuplet (J^P = 3/2+): Δ, Σ*, Ξ*, Ω — the SU(3) flavor decuplet.
cells.description: The eightfold-way baryon decuplet. The Ω- (sss, J^P=3/2+) was famously PREDICTED by Gell-Mann's SU(3) classification in 1962 with mass ≈ 1685 MeV before its 1964 discovery at mass 1672 MeV — the Mendeleev-style triumph of the hadron classification.
citations: Barnes et al. 1964, Phys. Rev. Lett. 12, 204 (Omega- discovery)

cells.content: J/ψ and other charmonium 1-- states: c-cbar bound states with J^P^C = 1--.
realized_examples: J/ψ (3097 MeV) — the canonical charmonium discovery (1974)
ψ(4040), ψ(4160), ψ(4415) — higher charmonium states
cells.description: The charmonium 1-- spectrum, discovered with the J/ψ in November 1974 (the 'November Revolution' that confirmed charm quark). Beautifully fits non-relativistic quark-antiquark potential models with QCD-motivated Cornell potential V(r) = -k/r + σr.
citations: Aubert et al. 1974, Phys. Rev. Lett. 33, 1404 (J/psi discovery, BNL)
Augustin et al. 1974, Phys. Rev. Lett. 33, 1406 (J/psi discovery, SLAC)
Eichten et al. 1980, Phys. Rev. D 21, 203 (Cornell potential)

cells.content: Υ and other bottomonium 1-- states: b-bbar bound states with J^P^C = 1--.
realized_examples: Υ(1S) (9460 MeV) — bottomonium ground state, discovered 1977
cells.description: The bottomonium 1-- spectrum. The Υ(1S) discovery in 1977 confirmed the bottom quark. The narrow widths below B-Bbar threshold and broader widths above match QCD potential-model predictions.
citations: Herb et al. 1977, Phys. Rev. Lett. 39, 252 (Upsilon discovery)

cells.content: Charm-baryon 1/2+ states: Λ_c, Σ_c, Ξ_c, Ω_c.
cells.description: Charm-baryon spectrum (one c quark + two light quarks). Established 1980s-1990s; refined by recent LHCb measurements.

cells.content: Bottom-baryon 1/2+ states: Λ_b, Σ_b, Ξ_b, Ω_b.
cells.description: Bottom-baryon spectrum. Largely a 2007-2017 LHCb / Tevatron discovery program.

cells.content: Doubly-charm baryon: Ξ_cc++ (3621 MeV), confirmed by LHCb 2017. The first doubly-heavy baryon. Doubly-bottom baryons Ξ_bb, Ω_bb predicted at ~10 GeV but not yet observed.
realized_examples: Ξ_cc++ (3621 MeV) — observed LHCb 2017
Ξ_bc, Ξ_bb, Ω_bb, B_c+ family — predicted but unobserved at various masses
cells.description: Doubly-heavy baryons require two heavy quarks (cc, bc, or bb). Ξ_cc++ confirmed in 2017 establishes the cc sector; cb and bb sectors are predicted but unobserved as of 2026. Active experimental search at LHCb and Belle II.
citations: Aaij et al. (LHCb) 2017, Phys. Rev. Lett. 119, 112001 (Xi_cc++)
Karliner, Rosner 2014, Phys. Rev. D 90, 094007 (theoretical predictions for doubly-heavy baryons)

cells.content: X(3872): the first confirmed exotic tetraquark candidate. Discovered Belle 2003. Mass 3872 MeV, J^P^C = 1++, narrow width.
realized_examples: X(3872) — Belle 2003, confirmed by BaBar, CDF, D0, LHCb, CMS
cells.description: The X(3872) — also known as χ_c1(3872) — is the most-studied exotic charmonium state. Its near-degeneracy with the D0-Dbar*0 threshold suggests a molecular interpretation; alternative diquark-antidiquark and conventional-charmonium-mixing interpretations remain debated. Universally accepted as a non-standard state.
citations: Choi et al. (Belle) 2003, Phys. Rev. Lett. 91, 262001
Maiani, Polosa, Riquer 2005, Phys. Rev. D 71, 014028 (diquark interpretation)

cells.content: Hidden-charm pentaquarks: P_c(4380), P_c(4312), P_c(4440), P_c(4457). Discovered LHCb 2015 and 2019 in Λ_b decays.
realized_examples: P_c(4312), P_c(4440), P_c(4457) — LHCb 2019 (refined spectroscopy)
P_cs(4459) — strange pentaquark, LHCb 2020
cells.description: Pentaquark states discovered by LHCb in 2015 and refined in 2019. Predicted by Maiani-Polosa-Riquer 2015 and others as hidden-charm 5-quark states. Established the pentaquark sector after decades of failed searches for light pentaquarks (e.g., the false Θ+ claim of 2003).
citations: Aaij et al. (LHCb) 2015, Phys. Rev. Lett. 115, 072001
Aaij et al. (LHCb) 2019, Phys. Rev. Lett. 122, 222001 (refined spectrum)
Maiani, Polosa, Riquer 2015, Phys. Lett. B 749, 289 (diquark model prediction)

cells.content: Charged charmonium / bottomonium-like tetraquark states: Z_c(3900), Z_c(4020), Z_b(10610), Z_b(10650). Definitively non-standard (charged charmonium implies tetraquark content).
realized_examples: Z_c(3900) — BESIII / Belle 2013
Z_c(4020) — BESIII 2013
cells.description: Charged tetraquark-like states. Their electric charge plus heavy-quark-content makes them unambiguously exotic (a charged hidden-charm state cannot be a pure c-cbar meson). Established the charged exotic sector.
citations: Ablikim et al. (BESIII) 2013, Phys. Rev. Lett. 110, 252001 (Zc(3900))

cells.content: Lowest scalar glueball: lattice QCD predicts mass ≈ 1500-1700 MeV with J^P^C = 0++. Mendeleev-style empty cell — predicted but not unambiguously identified. Candidate states f_0(1500) and f_0(1710) have significant glueball admixture but mix with conventional q-qbar states.
realized_examples: f_0(1500) — leading glueball candidate (disputed)
f_0(1710) — alternative glueball candidate (disputed)
Lattice prediction: m ≈ 1730 MeV (Morningstar-Peardon 1999), 1500 MeV (recent SU(3) lattice)
